Whenever I'm overwhelmed a bit, or feel there are too many things on my plate, I always try to come back to this quote:"
""
""Make things better. That's the job.""
""
"Not please everyone"
"Not to make things perfect"
"Not to make people happy"
""
"Just to make sure things are a bit better with you around. "
""
"It could go slower than you wish, as long as they are actually getting better :)"

During a 1:1, one of your engineers shares some tough feedback about another engineer on the team. What do you do?"
""
"It can be something like: "John is always complaining when you're not around and brings us down," or "John annoys me with his questions, he should be more independent"."
""
"If the engineer doesn't want to say it directly - because they feel it's not their job, or feel uncomfortable doing so - you're about to get stuck in limbo. "
""
"You can't say "John told me...." "
""
"You can't provide some "I heard from someone that…" fluff either."
""
"Sarah Drasner lists four qualities of a trusting team in 𝘌𝘯𝘨𝘪𝘯𝘦𝘦𝘳𝘪𝘯𝘨 𝘔𝘢𝘯𝘢𝘨𝘦𝘮𝘦𝘯𝘵 𝘧𝘰𝘳 𝘵𝘩𝘦 𝘙𝘦𝘴𝘵 𝘰𝘧 𝘜𝘴:"
""
"- They feel comfortable raising issues directly with each other. "
"- They share personal details with each other, and admit when they are having a bad day - to other team members, this is no big deal. "
"- They can debate with one another to find good outcomes. "
"- They are flexible with one another’s needs. "
""
"imo the first one is the most important one - when it's missing, the rest of her qualities can't really show up."
""
"I usually first try to convince the person who shared the feedback to talk directly with John. Honestly, it worked about 10% of the times..."
""
"If not, I try to push hard for permission to pass the feedback on, with attribution. "
""
"Sometimes it blows up. Person Y goes right back to John... But at least the feedback was said 🤷‍♂️"
""
"What's your approach?"
""
"------"
""

The 4 real reasons engineering managers don't do 1:1s. 'No time' isn't one of them:"
" "
"1. You think you'll have nothing to talk about."
"2. You are worried about what they will think."
"3. You are an introvert and don't like talking."
"4. It is not conventional in your organization."
" "
"If one of these is you - it was me too."
" "
"In my first job I avoided 1:1s. It wasn't conventional at my company, and I was worried about what my team would think. "
""
"In my next role I was afraid of them. I had to collect myself before each meeting. Some lasted 5–10 minutes, and some I canceled any time I could."
" "
"It took time, but now they're my best tool as a manager (and I enjoy them!)"
" "
"If one of those 4 is you, start simple:"
""
"1. Have a weekly meeting in the calendar. "
"Even 30 minutes is good. Some people think it's too much. I strongly disagree. You'll see the effects, try it. "
" "
"2. Don't be afraid of finishing early. "
"Even 5-7 minutes can be meaningful, if you make it so (not because you rush somewhere else though)"
""
"3. Don't cancel a meeting due to stupid reasons."
"It's the last meeting that should be canceled. Unless you are on vacation, or it's a total disaster - don't skip it. "
""
"4. Share about yourself - personal life, struggles."
"If you want to get to know the other side, you have to open up. This builds trust over time."
""
"5. Don't fall into the 'everyday' trap."
"It can be tempting to use the time to talk only about work stuff. While it's ok to use it like that, make sure to at least start with personal conversation."
""
"6. Talk once in a while about professional goals."
"It'll help you understand if the day-to-day tasks are aligned with the person's goals. If it's easy/boring for too long - you have to make some adjustments."
""

The 3 most common mistakes first-time engineering managers make (I made them all):"
""
"1. Not giving harsh feedback"
""
"At first, I completely skipped tough conversations. "
"I just tried to please everyone and be the nice guy. "
""
"When eventually I did get to them, they were very 'soft'. I prepared what I wanted to say in advance, but in real time, I chickened out."
""
"2. Giving a 💩 about the rest of the organization"
""
"I ignored what my manager and more experienced EMs thought was right. "
""
"Insisting on your vision and not backing up has its benefits, but it must be done respectfully. Especially as a first-time manager, you can't just start changing everything that was done before."
""
"And I tried... "
""
"In the end, it worked. But it alienated people and other teams, which hurt the organization as a whole."
""
"3. Not setting boundaries"
""
"Accepting mediocre work."
"When people didn't do a good enough job - I ignored it."
""
"As the manager, I should've been the one to set a standard for the team. It can be in code reviews, in feature designs, in how meetings are run.  "
""
"When you accept mediocre work, the strong engineers will be rightfully upset. "
""

11 promises every Engineering Manager should make (and actually keep):"
""
"After 9+ years of leading engineers, here are the promises I make to everyone who joins my team:"
""
"1. I won’t cancel our 1:1s. "
"Even when things are chaotic."
""
"2. I’ll care about your life, not just your output. "
"That means asking about your family, your hobbies, and your energy."
""
"3. When it’s time to move on, I’ll not hold you back. "
"Even if it'll be a big headache for me :) "
""
"4. I’ll always want to hear what’s broken - and how you’d fix it. "
""
"5. You’ll never be shut down for asking “why.” "
"Good questions are part of the job."
""
"6. You won’t just build things - you’ll know why they matter. "
"We connect our work to real outcomes."
""
"7. You won’t need to guess what matters. "
"And what is expected of you. I’ll make priorities and expectations clear."
""
"8. I’ll openly share my own mistakes. "
"So you know it’s safe to take risks."
""
"9. You’ll get the credit. "
"I'll work hard to ensure your impact is also noticed outside our team."
""
"10. You’ll talk to customers and stakeholders. "
"I’ll help you grow beyond just writing code."
""
"11. You’ll get real ownership. "
"And I’ll back your decisions when needed."
""
"What promise did I miss?"

Becoming an Engineering Manager doesn't make sense in 2026: "
"Over drinks a few weeks ago, a friend told me he'd been offered a promotion to an Engineering Manager role. "
""
"He planned to decline it, but wanted to hear my take first. "
""
"Until recently, my answer in such conversations was always “100% go for it”. "
""
"My logic was that it’s a super valuable experience, even if someone is not looking for the management career path. "
""
"I told every engineer that a couple of years as an EM would teach them valuable skills, and they could always go back afterward."
""
"This time, I finally agreed with him that he should not take that step:"
""
"𝟭. 𝗜𝘁'𝘀 𝗮 𝗯𝗮𝗱 𝘁𝗶𝗺𝗲 𝘁𝗼 𝗺𝗼𝘃𝗲 𝗮𝘄𝗮𝘆 𝗳𝗿𝗼𝗺 𝘁𝗲𝗰𝗵"
" "
"The pace of change in the last year has been completely crazy, and it's not stopping. Almost every part of our work looks different, and will continue to evolve."
" "
"My friend was afraid that as a manager, he'd have less time to experiment and adapt. Especially with a bigger team, you don't have much time to play around."
" "
"I could definitely relate to his fear."
""
"𝟮. 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗹𝗮𝗱𝗱𝗲𝗿 𝗶𝘀 𝘃𝗲𝗿𝘆 𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗽𝗲𝘁𝗶𝘁𝗶𝘃𝗲"
""
"Companies have been flattening for two years now. "
""
"Amazon increased its IC-to-manager ratio by 15%, and other companies followed. Fewer Director and VP roles to grow into. You can be a great EM for years and find yourself stuck. "
""
"As an IC, being excellent at building things can get you much further. "
""
"𝟯. 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗽𝗮𝘆 𝗶𝘀 𝗹𝗼𝘄𝗲𝗿"
" "
"My friend was offered a bump with the promotion to EM, but the total compensation was less than the offers he received for Senior/Staff Engineer at other startups."
" "
"The assumption has always been that management pays more. It does at the same company. But across the industry, being a Staff engineer is better paid - those engineers are in huge demand."
""
"𝗦𝗼 𝘄𝗵𝘆 𝗮𝗺 𝗜 𝘀𝘁𝗶𝗹𝗹 𝗮𝗻 𝗘𝗠?"
" "
"Rationally, I believe that being an IC is a smarter choice in 2026, I know I would enjoy it less."
" "
"If you are a senior engineer, I wouldn't recommend management right now. I would wait a couple of years to see how things will look like."
" "
"BUT - if your gut tells you to do it (and not your brain), if it's truly a path you want to pursue  - then go for it!"
" "
"Or, at least join 17,000+ engineering managers getting one practical article per week to have a better feel for what the job is like 🙃"
"newsletter.manager.dev"
""
"Oh, and if you do want to find such job, leadjobs.dev is the best place for you! (yep, a project of mine)"
"https://lnkd.in/dNW8j3U4

7 software engineering lessons you learn only after breaking production:"
" "
"𝟭. 𝗜𝘁'𝘀 𝗔𝗹𝘄𝗮𝘆𝘀 𝗥𝗲𝗹𝗮𝘁𝗲𝗱 "
""
"I lost count of how many times production broke right after I deployed something, and my first instinct was: "That's totally not related to what I touched." "
""
"Surprise… It's almost always related. "
""
"Don't spend an hour proving your change is innocent - roll back, stabilize, then debug."
" "
"𝟮. 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗕𝗮𝗰𝗸𝘂𝗽 𝗟𝗮𝘄 "
""
"How many of you have actually tried the restore option on your managed database? Not "I know it exists" - actually clicked through, watched it run, and confirmed your data came back? "
""
"Backups aren't real until you've restored from them."
" "
"𝟯. 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗟𝗼𝗴𝗴𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗟𝗮𝘄 "
""
"No matter how many times I started a repo with the intention of writing perfect logs, when an incident happened, something was always missing. "
""
"And now Cursor & Claude cause the opposite problem - too many super verbose and confusing logs."
" "
"𝟰. 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗥𝗼𝗹𝗹𝗯𝗮𝗰𝗸 𝗣𝗹𝗮𝗻 𝗟𝗮𝘄 "
""
"If you touch data, you need a quick, tested rollback plan (never mind how small the migration or how minor the DB change seems ). The keyword is 𝘵𝘦𝘴𝘵𝘦𝘥. A rollback plan you haven't actually run has a 50/50 chance to break."
" "
"𝟱. 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗗𝗲𝗽𝗲𝗻𝗱𝗲𝗻𝗰𝘆 𝗟𝗮𝘄 "
""
"As an engineer in a small company, adding an external API is so easy. You log in, get an API key, boom - works. "
""
"But rarely does anyone ask: "What happens when this thing goes down?" "
""
"Every external dependency will fail."
" "
"𝟲. 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗙𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝗘𝘆𝗲𝘀 𝗟𝗮𝘄 "
""
"A few years ago, I destroyed the company's DB after a very stupid mistake. "
""
"Now this rule is burned into my brain: if I have ANY doubt whether what I'm about to do is 1000% safe, I ask someone to look at it with me. "
""
"Half the time you catch the mistake yourself just by explaining it out loud."
" "
"𝟳. 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗧𝗲𝗺𝗽𝗼𝗿𝗮𝗿𝘆 𝗙𝗶𝘅 𝗟𝗮𝘄 "
""
"So many times I implemented V1 of a project, just the bare bones, with tons of hacks, planning to improve things in V2. "
""
"90% of the cases, the time never arrived. "
""
"There's a big difference between "this is simple and limited" and "this is held together with duct tape.""
""
"----------"
""

"newsletter.manager.dev

There's a law that says your software will look exactly like your org chart. It's from 1967, and one company used it to ship 30x faster:"
""
"It's called Conway's Law."
""
""Organizations produce designs which are copies of the communication structures of these organizations.""
""
"In plain English: your system architecture mirrors how your teams are structured."
" "
"For example, A SaaS company has separate frontend and backend teams. Because these teams operate separately, their communication structure affects the product architecture:"
""
"- The frontend team builds a UI that expects data in a certain format."
"- The backend team builds APIs based on their own assumptions."
"- The API responses don’t match exactly what the frontend expects, requiring extra transformations."
"- Over time, the SaaS app ends up with extra layers of glue code and inefficiencies because the teams didn’t collaborate closely."
""
"Most Engineering Managers are familiar with it - but people rarely talk about the 𝗜𝗻𝘃𝗲𝗿𝘀𝗲 𝗖𝗼𝗻𝘄𝗮𝘆 𝗠𝗮𝗻𝗲𝘂𝘃𝗲𝗿."
""
"You can basically flip it to your own advantage. "
""
"A great example is what Flo Health Inc. did: "
""
"They struggled with 3-week app store release cycles for their mobile app. So they put in each team just one iOS and one Android engineer, and 2-4 backend engineers. By pushing more functionality to the backend, 𝘁𝗵𝗲𝘆 𝗰𝗮𝗻 𝗿𝗲𝗹𝗲𝗮𝘀𝗲 𝟮𝟬-𝟯𝟬 𝘁𝗶𝗺𝗲𝘀 𝗽𝗲𝗿 𝗱𝗮𝘆 instead of every few weeks. "
""
"Eugene and Nihan shared the full story in a guest post for manager.dev:"
"https://lnkd.in/dpQv-_8Z"
""
"I write about how the best engineering teams actually operate. Weekly, free: newsletter.manager.dev

A surprising lesson from an engineering manager at Amazon: "
""
"Stop using words like 'significant' or 'annoying' - Weasel words."
""
"This is the 2nd lesson Gilad Naor shared in his manager.dev guest post: https://lnkd.in/dtXPt4EK"
""
"--------------------------------------------"
""
""In Amazon, I learned to never use "Weasel words"."
""
"I stopped using terms like "significant", "low latency" or "annoying." Instead, I used specific metrics, like "P99 latency jumped 375% from 12ms to 45ms." "
""
"This required more upfront work, but led to discussing reality as it is, not as I imagined it to be."
""
"I was working on 𝗺𝘆 𝗳𝗶𝗿𝘀𝘁 𝟲-𝗣𝗮𝗴𝗲𝗿, 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗶𝘁 𝘄𝗮𝘀 𝗵𝗮𝗿𝗱 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗳𝗿𝘂𝘀𝘁𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗴. "
""
"My manager connected me with a mentor who taught me how to write like an Amazonian. She taught me all about weasel words, how to identify them, and how to avoid them."
""
"But what sticks with me to this day is actually all the 1:1 meetings with my manager. "
""
"I learned that weasel words don't only appear in business documents. They also appeared in how I gave feedback to my team members. I learned to do the homework so that I could give specific feedback."
""
"I stopped saying "You are too blunt in meetings." Instead, I said "In the meeting with Christopher you cut him off when he started suggesting the React-based solution. We missed an opportunity to check more approaches. I also noticed that Christopher stopped contributing to other meetings.""
""
"This feedback is factual and thus indisputable. It is really so easy for the other side to understand. It is the first step to actionable advice.""
""
"Every week I share one lesson from being an engineering manager. 17,000+ EMs read it. Join them 👇 "
"newsletter.manager.dev

In 7 years as an engineering manager, I tried to stop supporting ~10 different features. I succeeded just once:"
""
"Every single time, there were 3 super vocal users who built their entire workflow around a behavior we never intended to support."
""
"There's a name for this - Hyrum's Law:"
""With a sufficient number of users of an API, it does not matter what you promise in the contract: all observable behaviors of your system will be depended on by somebody.""
""
"People hate changes... And the solution is almost always to have toggleable settings that will let the old timers restore 'their way of doing things'."
""
"Over time, these compounds and creates a super complex product, both for the end users and for the engineers who maintain it. "
""
"I haven't found a good solution to it so far :) "
""
"P.S.: That's law number 8 in the '13 software engineers laws', here are the rest of them:"
"https://lnkd.in/d-jfTBZQ

"In January 2025, we killed sprint planning, standups, and backlog grooming. My engineers said it was the most productive year of their careers""
" "
"I met Matthew Littlehale in my engineering management course. When he told me his company had dropped Scrum and fully adopted Shape Up (and had already run 8 cycles), I asked him to write a guest post about it."
" "
"Here's the gist in his words:"
""
"Shape Up replaces 2-week sprints with 6-week(ish) cycles, followed by a 2-week cooldown."
" "
"Leading up to a cycle, anyone in the organization can bring a pitch. A pitch outlines a business problem that needs to be solved, not the actual feature to develop. Leadership then decides how much time they're willing to invest in each problem (the appetite for it). "
""
"Then teams are formed around the selected projects. Each team has full autonomy to decide how they operate - how often they meet, how detailed they create tickets."
" "
"𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗯𝗶𝗴𝗴𝗲𝘀𝘁 𝘀𝗵𝗶𝗳𝘁 𝘄𝗮𝘀 𝗳𝗼𝗰𝘂𝘀. "
""
"Any request that comes through outside of the pitch process is triaged before any individual engineer is interrupted by it. Is this request more important than the projects already prioritized? Sometimes it is - but the process protects engineers from interruptions that would normally take them away from their project."
" "
"And the cooldown - engineers themselves lead what gets done. Tech debt they've discovered, documentation, smaller requests - whatever just needs to get done that doesn't fit into a project."
""
"The full story (including what didn't work)  is in last week's article 👇 "
"https://lnkd.in/d_YBbXJC

24 שעות לפני תחילת המלחמה סיימתי את המרתון הראשון שלי. כמה מילים על מה ששינה לי פוראבר את היחסים עם ריצה (חובה קריאה למי שאי פעם רצה לרוץ):"
""
"זה התחיל באפריל 2023."
""
"3 שנים חלמתי לעשות טריאתלון, אבל אף פעם לא לקחתי את החלום ברצינות. "
""
"שבוע אחרי החתונה שלנו, מה שחשבנו שהיו הקאות מאלכוהול לא נגמרו כמה ימים וגילינו שענבר בהיריון ראשון 😅"
""
"עכשיו היה לי דד ליין ברור - עד נובמבר 2023 אני רוצה לסיים את הטריאתלון. "
""
"את אסף מצאתי מחיפוש בגוגל  של "הכנה לטריאתלון תל אביב". אחת המציאות המוצלחות שלי. "
""
"רוב הזמן לא הבנתי על מה הוא חופר באימונים, מה זה "לרוץ לפי דופק ולא לפי קצב". "
"הוא כל הזמן היה אומר לי להאט, והתשובה שלי הייתה קבועה: "אני בא להתאמן כדי להוציא אנרגיות, אני יכול יותר מהר". "
""
"בטריאתלון, יש 1.5 ק"מ שחייה, 40 ק"מ אופניים, 10 ק"מ ריצה. ריצה היה הצד החזק שלי, עשיתי כבר כמה מירוצים של 10 ק"מ, אז לא הרגשתי הרבה צורך באימונים. "
""
"מפה לשם, עברו 5 חודשים, וסיימתי בהצלחה טריאתלון אולימפי באליפות ישראל באשדוד. הריצה הייתה סיוט, אבל סיימתי... "
""
" אבל הסיפור רק מתחיל כאן. "
""
"לקחתי הפסקה ארוכה אחרי הלידה של דן, יותר משנה ללא ספורט. אבל ידעתי שזה זמני, ושהיעד הבא הוא מרתון שלם. "
""
"חזרתי להתאמן אצל אסף לאט לאט, ובאפריל שעבר, גילינו שענבר בהיריון שני, והיה לי שוב דד ליין :) (ספוילר - לא הספקתי, אלון נולד חודש לפני המרתון). "
""
"כיוונתי למרתון תל אביב. קל"ב, מזג טוב, אחלה אווירה. "
""
"והפעם, ידעתי שאני חייב להשקיע קצת יותר באימונים אם אני רוצה לסיים את הריצה בלי להיפצע. "
"אז התחלתי להקשיב לטיפים."
""
"הפואנטה היא שאצל רוב האנשים, ה"כושר" של הרגליים ושל הלב לא מסונכרנים. "
""
"כשאנחנו רצים בדופק גבוה, הרגליים בסדר, אבל הלב באובר מאמץ. באופן לחלוטין לא אינטואיטיבי - אם תריצו לאט יותר, בדופק הנכון, הקצב שלכם ישתפר מהר יותר. "
""
"רוב האנשים שונאים לרוץ כי הם רצים עם דופק בשמיים וסובלים מכל רגע. אבל אין מי שלא אוהב לעשות הליכות נכון?"
""
"לקח לי הרבה יותר מדי זמן אשכרה לעמוד בזה. תמיד תמיד גנבתי קצת, ורצתי יותר מהר ממה שאני אמור, כי ככה המוח שלי היה מחווט לקבל סיפוק. מה יש לי לרוץ בקצב 6 דקות לק"מ, משעמם, לא?"
""
"אבל וואלה, בסוף חדר לי למוח. "
""
"את החצי מרתון בעמק החולה סיימתי ב1:59, 5:40 לק"מ, כשאני מרגיש מעולה. את ה-3 ק"מ האחרונים סיימתי ב-5 דקות/ק"מ פשוט כי היו כוחות."
""
"חודש לפני המרתון האימונים קצת השתבשו לי אחרי הלידה של אלון, אבל אם יש משהו שלמדתי, זה שאין מה למהר. מרתון ראשון מבין הרבה. "
""
"הקשבתי לגוף (ולאסף), והלכתי על קצב של 6:30 דקות לק"מ (במקום 6:15 שתיכננתי). סיימתי ב4:36. "
""
"על החוויות והמחשבות מהריצה עצמה אפרט בהזדמנות אחרת, הפואנטה היום היא:"
""
"3 שנים של אימונים. טריאתלון. מרתון. "
"ל-ל-א   פ-צ-י-ע-ה   א-ח-ת!"
"אתם קולטים? לא פספסתי אפילו אימון אחד בגלל כאבים!"
""
"ובכנות, זה הכל בזכות הטכניקה והמנטליות שאסף חפר לי עליהן. "
"כי מסתבר שצריך ללמוד לרוץ."
""
"זה לא פוסט ממומן, אסף אפילו לא יודע שכתבתי אותו (ולא נראה לי שיש לו לינקדאין). "
""
"זה פשוט רצון לפרגן למאמן מדהים, ולבעל עסק בישראל. "
""
"אז אם אתם גרים במרכז, וחלמתם לרוץ או לעשות טריאתלון, ובא לכם ליווי - זה האתר שלו:"
"https://lnkd.in/dY_r7Wgs"

""

""
"They know when to push back (and how to do it nicely). "
""
"And NONE of them (I currently manage 3) let their ego control them, or make me feel like they can do my job better. "
""
"I even had the luck to manage my own former EM, and he was super supportive. "
""
"I feel that the EM job is so hard, that people who step back into IC roles understand your place, and have a lot of empathy toward your challenges. "
""
"So to all ex-EMs out there - thank you for making our lives easier ❤️

The 13 software engineering laws every engineering leader must know: "
""
"1. Hofstadter's law: It always takes longer than you expect, even when you take into account Hofstadter's law."
""
"2. Cunningham's law: The best way to get the right answer on the internet is not to ask a question, but to post the wrong answer."
""
"3. Hyrum's law: With sufficient users of an API, all observable behaviors will be depended on by somebody, regardless of what the contract promises."
""
"4. Goodhart's law: When a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure."
""
"5. Conway's law: Organizations produce designs which are copies of their communication structures."
""
"6. Price's law: 50% of the work is done by the square root of the total number of people."
""
"7. Zawinski's law: Every program attempts to expand until it can read mail."
""
"8. Brooks' law: Adding manpower to a late software project makes it later."
""
"9. Ringelmann effect: Individual productivity decreases as group size increases."
""
"10. Sturgeon's law: 90% of everything is crap."
""
"11. Gilb's law: Anything you need to quantify can be measured in some way that is superior to not measuring it at all."
""
"12. Parkinson's law: Work expands to fill the available time."
""
"13. Murphy's law: Anything that can go wrong will go wrong."
" "
"Save for later and share with your manager 🙃"
""
"Which is your favorite?"

הרגע המביך בחיים שלי היה בבה"ד 1 לפני 10 שנים."
"עליתי לארץ בגיל 3 מברית המועצות, שני ההורים שלי נולדו וגדלו שם. "
""
"הם ניהלו אורך חיים יהודי, אבל כמו כל יוצאי בריה"מ, אהבו לציין את נוביי גוד. "
""
"גם בארץ, זה נשאר חג מאוד משמעותי עבורינו, נטו כמסורת משפחתית. לא היה אף חג שפספסתי, תמיד היה ברור שזה חג שעושים בבית עם כל המשפחה."
""
"יצאתי לקורס קצינים בנובמבר 2016, ועוד מההתחלה שאלתי אם אוכל לצאת לחג הבייתה. "
""
"הגיע אמצע דצמבר, והחליטו באופן גורף שאין יציאות לחג."
""
"התבאסתי, אבל החלטתי להרים נוביי גוד לכל הגדוד. זה היה מוצאי שבת, וביקשתי אם אפשר לשריין אולם מ23:00 עד 00:15, ועם העזרה של המפק"צ והמ"פ יצאה אחלה הפקה. "
""
"הגיעו נראה לי ~150 איש מ-3 פלוגות. "
""
"התחלתי מחידון kahoot אישי נושא פרסים, ואז תחרות קצרה בין הפלוגות."
""
"אחרי זה הוקרן סטנדאפ קצר של גיורא זינגר על רוסים, וכולם היו באחלה אווירה. "
""
"לקראת הסוף, הקרנתי ברכה מוקלטת מהמ"פ שלי. הוא באמת היה תותח - תמך בארגון, והיה היחיד שזרם (ביקשתי מכל המ"פים והמג"ד). "
""
"ופה נפלתי. "
""
"הברכה שלו הוקרנה מיד אחרי הסטנדאפ. הוא הקליט אותה בסלפי תוך כדי נסיעה, והפרצוף שלו הוקרן על מסך מלאנתלפים אינץ'. "
""
"זה היה נראה מגוחך. כולם פשוט נשברו מצחוק במשך כל הברכה. הסתלבטו עליו רצח, וגם זרקו כמה בדיחות לא מאוד נעימות. "
""
"המ"פ כמובן לא היה בקהל. צחקתי עם כולם, והמשכתי הלאה בחיי. "
""
"אחרי כמה ימים, הוא קרא לי לשיחה בצד. לא קישרתי אפילו בין הדברים. מסתבר שהמפק"צ שלי היה נוכח, וצילם חלק גדול מהערב, וכמובן שהראה למ"פ. "
""
"לכאורה, זה נראה שטויות. אז קצת צחקו עליו, מה יש. "
""
"אני עדיין זוכר את מה שהוא אמר לי (פחות או יותר):"
""חיכיתי כמה ימים כי קיוויתי שתבוא להתנצל. יש לך אומץ לארגן אירוע לכל הגדוד, ולבייש אדם בפומבי, אבל לבקש סליחה גדול עליך? הסרטון הזה היה הרגע הכי משפיל שחוויתי מכל תקופתי בבה"ד 1. אתה כנראה הולך להיות קצין, אבל תהיה אדם קודם"."
""
"וזה הרגע המביך שלי. אני עומד מולו, ופשוט לא יודע מה להגיד. מלמלתי סליחה כלשהי וברחתי. "
""
"אני באמת מרגיש שהרגע הזה שינה משהו בהתנהלות שלי, הרבה יותר מכל חוויה אחרת בבה"ד. "
""
"אני אפילו לא יודע להצביע מה בדיוק. הוא פשוט קופץ לי מדי פעם בראש כשאני מרגיש שלא כיבדתי אדם אחר, או שעשיתי משהו לא נעים מאחורי הגב. "
""
"מן תזכורת כזאת פשוט להיות בן אדם.

A lesson every proper software engineer learned through blood and tears:"
""
"No matter how small the migration or how minor the DB change seems - if you touch data, you need a quick, tested rollback plan."
""
"Adding a column? Have a migration ready to remove it, and a PR to revert the related code changes. "
""
"Inserting data? Be prepared to delete the exact rows you inserted. "
""
"Deleting data? Copy it somewhere safe first. "
""
"Changing a column type or constraint? Know exactly how to reverse it without data loss."
""
"The keyword here is 𝘵𝘦𝘴𝘵𝘦𝘥. A rollback plan you haven’t actually run has a 50/50 chance to break."
""

One of the hardest things as an EM is to balance between what your company needs and what your engineers need. Even the most basic "Who does what" is way more complex than it seems:"
""
"It sounds so simple. Match the ‘resources’ to the ‘missions’. Every spreadsheet pusher should be able to do it."
""
"That’s how teams fail and engineer careers are ruined."
""
"There are 3 layers in how I’ve seen managers approach this challenge."
""
"𝗟𝗮𝘆𝗲𝗿 #𝟭 - 𝗘𝗳𝗳𝗶𝗰𝗶𝗲𝗻𝗰𝘆 (𝘄𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝘆𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗽𝗮𝗻𝘆 𝗻𝗲𝗲𝗱𝘀)"
""
"What needs to be done RIGHT NOW."
""
"For every need, you take the most capable person in that area. The same people keep doing the same kind of projects again and again, as it’s the most efficient use of their time."
""
"𝗟𝗮𝘆𝗲𝗿 #𝟮 - 𝗔𝗱𝘃𝗮𝗻𝗰𝗲𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁 & 𝗰𝗵𝗮𝗹𝗹𝗲𝗻𝗴𝗲 (𝘄𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝘆𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝗲𝗻𝗴𝗶𝗻𝗲𝗲𝗿𝘀 𝗻𝗲𝗲𝗱)"
""
"Trying to take into account your engineers’ career aspirations."
""
"So if a mid level engineer wants to become a manager someday, they’ll get more opportunities to lead others, mentor new hires, etc. "
""
"The same holds for trying to keep the engineers challenged, providing them growth opportunities (often at the price of efficiency)."
""
"You also need to take into account if there is a promotion on the line. "
""
"Especially at more senior levels, engineers will need to get the right opportunities to demonstrate their readiness."
""
"Some well-intending managers lean into this one too much - letting engineers work on sexy side projects that have nothing to do with the company’s needs (but are done in a ‘super modern stack’ that keeps the engineers happy)."
""
"Most of your probably experienced it - the play between efficiency and people growth is super tricky. You cannot lean too hard to either direction, and you constantly need to reassess and update your decisions."
""
"𝗟𝗮𝘆𝗲𝗿 #𝟯 - 𝗗𝘂𝗿𝗮𝗯𝗶𝗹𝗶𝘁𝘆/𝗙𝗹𝗲𝘅𝗶𝗯𝗶𝗹𝗶𝘁𝘆 (𝘄𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝘆𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝘁𝗲𝗮𝗺 𝗻𝗲𝗲𝗱𝘀)"
""
"Sometimes, neither efficiency nor people advancement is the right decision."
""
"For example, let’s take a shitty legacy system your team maintains. You have one engineer who is familiar with it, and handles most of the noise there. They don’t care that much about it, and don’t mind doing it alone."
""
"Everybody else hates it, and don’t want to touch it even with a stick. "
""
"What do you do?"
""
"Maybe you can afford to depend on a single engineer. But maybe not - what happens if she takes a vacation, or moves to another team?"

""
"אז מסתבר שהשלב הראשון, של עוסק פטור (עד הכנסה שנתית של 120 אלף) זה באמת שטויות. חצי שעה באיזה אתר והכל מוכן. "

"זהו! "

Give your engineers a kingdom: in too many teams, everything goes through the Engineering Manager. You divide the work, and your people execute."
" "
"This might work if you grew within the team and know everything, or your team is relatively junior. "
" "
"When your engineers grow (or when you arrive on a new team), it becomes much less practical."
" "
"It's time to give your engineers a kingdom! "
""
"What "owning" a kingdom looks like"
" "
"The key point here is decision-making. The engineer responsible for the kingdom should be the one making the decisions about it."
" "
"Some practical examples:"
" "
"An application kingdom - Working with PM+designer, understanding the roadmap and helping them prioritize. Being on top of customer usage. Being the technical go-to person."
" "
"A microservice kingdom - Monitoring ongoing health (latency? memory leaks? dependencies?). Deciding on a technical-debt roadmap. Being responsible for docs."
" "
"A 3rd party kingdom (like a weather API) - Leading the joint work with the other company. Being on top of release notes and upgrades."
" "
"A tool kingdom (Datadog/Sentry, Kafka/PubSub, Argo Workflows) - Being the expert and spreading knowledge. Working with other teams to create common guidelines."
""
"Doing it right:"
""
"First, think about whether it's needed. In some teams, it just happens naturally. If there are no knowledge bottlenecks and you don't feel overwhelmed, maybe you don't need it at all. "
""
"If you decide to go that you - make sure to not have 30+ kingdoms, spreading your people too thin. My recommendation is 1 or 2 per engineer. Choose ones that take the most time from you."
""
"A kingdom is just "ownership areas", but this term can have a positive effect. "
""
"Being an 'owner' might be seen as a chore. When you talk about a kingdom, it can be perceived better.

מה לעזאזל עשיתי ב-2025 ולמה יש לי 5 משרות בשנה בלינקדאין?"
"על העובדה שהתפטרתי לפני שנה ויצאתי לחפש את עצמי אני מניח שחפרתי מספיק. "
""
"אני מחלק (בראש שלי) את החוויות והמסקנות מהשנה לשלושה תחומים: להיות יזם, שכיר, ועצמאי (שזה החלק הכי מעניין). "
""
"1. קריירה כיזם "
""
"אמל"ק - לא בשבילי. זה פשוט קשה מדי מנטלית (עבורי). "
""
"הקבלה שנתתי לחבר: דמיין שאתה משחק במשחק מחשב, וכל פעם שאתה מת אתה מאבד את כל מה שצברת ומתחיל לחלוטין מההתחלה. "
""
"בתור שכיר, המחיר ל'פסילה' הוא מאוד נמוך. אתה תקבל את אותו השכר ואותן האופציות, לא משנה כמה טעויות תעשה (בהנחה ואתה סה"כ סבבה). "
""
"הלחץ התמידי, אי ודאות (בעיקר), הכישלונות החוזרים - ממש פגעו ביכולת שלי לתפקד. לא הייתי שותף טוב. "
"ניסיתי שני מיזמים שנגדעו מהר, ועוד אחד שהקדשתי לו חצי שנה. "
""
"האפסייד פשוט לא שווה את זה מבחינתי - מעדיף חיים שפויים מאשר אופציה לעשות קופה. "
"  "
"2. קריירה כשכיר"
""
"אמל"ק - מאוד בשבילי. "
""
"במהלך תקופת היזמות, הבנתי כמה היה לי טוב כראש צוות בחברה. "
""
"התגעגעתי לניהול של אנשים, לעבודה עם צוות, לעבוד בארגון שזז ביחד למטרה גדולה. לזה שיש מישהו שאני סומך עליו לקבל החלטות גדולות, ולא הכל תלוי בי. "

In my last job search, I specifically targeted companies with 200-500 employees. I believe that's where an EM has the most opportunity to grow:"
""
"It's a bit counter intuitive. "
""
"Many EMs believe that bigger companies equals more advancement opportunities, but I think it's the opposite. "
""
"In big companies, you have a lot of people competing for those roles. People who've been there for years, and have stronger political capital than you do. Proving yourself is also much harder - projects take longer, and each team owns a small part of the pie. "
""
"In smaller companies, it's the opposite: easier to prove yourself, less competition for promotions. "
""
"In my previous company (Series C-D startup, ~100 people), I grew from engineer to director in 3 years - something I believe would have never happened in a bigger company. "
""
"The 2nd advantage of a 200-500 people company is that you also have role models to learn from (at least that's what I looked for when interviewing). Directors who've been in my shoes and can teach me a thing or two. "
""
"---------------------------------"
""
"For the first time EM, I believe a 1-100 employees company can offer the fastest growth (if that's what you optimize for). "
""
"You can see in the graphs the relative amount of open jobs in Senior EM+ roles - an indication those roles are not filled internally. "
""
"For more experienced EMs, my suggestions is to look for a great 100-1000 company to take your next step. "

The 'is-even' npm package has 93k weekly downloads. That's not even the crazy part - all it does is importing 'is-odd' (which has 250k weekly download) and negating it..."
""
"The CTO of a startup I worked at hated dependencies. We worked with some 3D calculations (software for drones), and he was writing tens of mathematical functions himself. "
""
"He insisted that even though it’s slower, he at least understands every part and can fix any bug that will pop up, and wouldn’t depend on anyone else for critical parts of our software."
""
"I used to make fun of that paranoia, until I read about some crazy stories:"
""
"The developer of left-pad took it down from NPM, breaking the builds of Facebook, Spotify, Netflix, and many more. It’s basically an 11-line for loop that adds spaces to a string."
""
"With packages, you are also much more vulnerable to security incidents (and need to spend a significant amount of time chasing updates). "
""
"In most smaller companies, there is no vetting process for packages (unlike for vendors) - every engineer does as they please."
""
"With LLMs, it’s easier to both get into this mess and get out of it: it’s much easier to install an unneeded dependency by mistake, but it’s also quicker to implement ‘known’ solutions from scratch."
""
"Curious to know how other engineers approach this balance?

Engineers are analytical people. They focus on actions, technical skills, and processes. Feelings are for softies. The reality is that your nervous system is part of your engineering tools, and is probably even more important than any other skill."
""
"Nobody can operate at their best when their body thinks they are in danger. "
""
"When your engineers are tense before a standup, exhausted after a sprint, or frustrated because of poor decisions, it has a huge impact on basically everything."
""
"Feelings are signals."
""
"The dashboard of your car has indicators to show when something is going on. Your body has feelings. Leaving them unaddressed can get very expensive :) "
""
"One of your jobs as an EM is to actually listen to those feelings and help your engineers understand what needs to be changed."
""
"When you ask someone, “How did you feel this week?” and give space for a meaningful answer (by staying silent), interesting things will come up."
""

"The older and bigger the company -> the slower it is (duh)"

""
"As a burned Jira user, I know it's annoying, but I was not sure it actually has an impact on development speed. "

"I analyzed data from 4.5k+ engineers and 200+ companies using Jira and Linear, and the data is pretty clear:"
""
"Companies using Linear ship 86-91% more code than those on Jira."

"Still, it's quite a big difference. "

I've recently read a superb article by Will Larson, who got back to significant coding as CTO (after a decade). "
"Here are his 4 guidelines:"
""
"1.  Never contribute to something that is 𝘁𝗿𝘂𝗹𝘆 𝘁𝗶𝗺𝗲 𝘀𝗲𝗻𝘀𝗶𝘁𝗶𝘃𝗲 unless it’s a very constrained thing that I can solve end-to-end 𝘵𝘰𝘥𝘢𝘺. When that isn’t the case, I’m usually going to slow things down despite trying to help."
""
"2. Prioritize projects that are 𝗵𝗮𝗿𝗱 𝗳𝗼𝗿 𝘁𝗲𝗮𝗺𝘀 𝘁𝗼 𝗴𝗲𝘁 𝘁𝗼, but are obviously valuable over time. For example, technical debt clean up, small user-requested features, or missing instrumentation."
""
"Infrequently take on a 𝘀𝘁𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗲𝗴𝗶𝗰 𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗽𝗮𝗻𝘆 𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗷𝗲𝗰𝘁 𝘄𝗵𝗶𝗰𝗵 𝗱𝗼𝗲𝘀𝗻’𝘁 𝗵𝗮𝘃𝗲 𝗮𝗻 𝗼𝘄𝗻𝗲𝗿, and which I am able to complete 𝘮𝘺𝘴𝘦𝘭𝘧. A real example of this has been writing our first pass of a new Interactive Voice Response built on an AI agent, which was clearly valuable but difficult to prioritize over the team’s existing work."
""
"3. Hold myself to a 𝗵𝗶𝗴𝗵𝗲𝗿 𝗯𝗮𝗿 than I would hold others in terms of fully releasing my software, monitoring its release, and solving bugs it creates. If I don’t have time to do those things, then I am stealing time from the team rather than helping them."
""
"4. Err towards 𝗶𝗺𝗽𝗹𝗲𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗳𝗲𝗲𝗱𝗯𝗮𝗰𝗸 𝗶𝗻 𝗽𝘂𝗹𝗹 𝗿𝗲𝗾𝘂𝗲𝘀𝘁𝘀, even if I consider it generally neutral. The cost of giving feedback to me is so high for folks, that I’m responsible for going out of my way to incorporate it when it is given."

"אין אנשים שלמנים יותר מהורים תל אביביים לילד ראשון. אנחנו שמים 5 (פלוס) אלף על גן פרטי, מה לא נשפוך עוד קצת שיצא ילד מוצלח יותר?  "
""
" גם לי כמובן בא שדן יהיה תותח על, וקראתי איפשהו שבשנים הראשונות יש הרבה נוירונים שמתחווטים להם, אז כדאי להשקיע.  "
""
"ולא נראה לי שכל הטיולים שלנו לגינה מפתחים את השכל. נדנדה, מגלשה, נדנדה, מגלשה. בקושי בגרות יוציא ככה.  "
""
"- בעיה - צ'ק✅ "
"- תחום - צ'ק✅ "
"- כסף - צ'ק✅  "
""
"אז הנה רעיון פשוט: עטיפה לצאט גיפיטי, שעוזרת לך לעשות פעילויות מגניבות ב-0 מאמץ. אתה מתאר את הילד בכמה מילים, ולנו יהיה מאגר מטריף, וכשתבקש בווטסאפ נשלח לך כל פעם פעילות .  "
""
"עוד לפני שהתפטרתי, היו לי שני שותפים פוטנציאליים. זרקתי להם את הכיוון, והיה נשמע שיש עניין.  "
""
"עם קרסר, אמור להיות שטויות להרים (זה היה קצת לפני שלאבאבל ובייס היו סבירים).  "
""
"אז במקביל לפיתוח, החלטנו לדבר עם הלקוחות העתידיים! אספנו תשובות לסקר מ-100 איש, ועלינו ל-8 שיחות זום עם אמהות (משום מה אבות לא שיתפו פעולה).  "
""
"סיגנל חלש.  "
""
"אמא אחת אמרה יפה: "
""זה כמו אוכל בריא. בוא, ברור לך שאני רוצה שהילד שלי יאכל אוכל בריא. אבל למי יש כוח עכשיו אחרי יום עבודה להתעסק בזה.  אוכלים פסטה ושניצל וזה מה יש. שיתגלש מצידי גם שעתיים כל עוד יש לי שקט".  "
""
"אבל כבר היינו באמצע הפיתוח... ורצינו להשיק V1  ולא נעים, יש התחייבות מול השותפים האחרים.  "
""
"ובתחילת הדרך ידענו שזה רק ניסיון אקראי - היו לנו עוד כל מיני רעיונות בתחום (לדוגמה לעשות קצת סדר בהודעות והתמונות שבאות מהגנים, אולי מנוי לערכת פעילויות פיזית שמגיעה כל שבוע הבייתה, או משלוח אוטומטי של חיתולים ומגבונים שיודע מה נגמר לך וחוסך את ההתעסקות).  "
""
"איכשהו, פשוט יצא לי האוויר. כולה 3-4 שבועות ופשוט לא היה לי כיף לעבוד על זה. "
""
"גם הפסקתי להאמין בפרויקט, גם משהו בדינמיקה עם הצוות לא עבד. "
""
"לפני כמה שנים, כנראה שהייתי ממשיך כי "לא נעים לי". למדתי עם הזמן פשוט לחתוך כשאני מרגיש שזה לא נכון עבורי.  "
""
"אז סוף פברואר החלטתי שדי, הגיע הזמן לעבור לניסיון אחר.  חודש אחד מאחורי, שמונה נותרו."
""
"  ---------- "
""
" - תודה ל- 🕸 Daniel Aranias  על הפוש לפוסט ההמשך :) "
"- עדיין חושב שיש מלא מקום לשיפורים בתחום, אם מישהו עובד על זה אני מתנדב להיות טסטר

קיבלתי מלא עצות סותרות על יזמות, כמו  - "אל תתפטר עד שיש לך הכנסות", אל מול "אם אתה באמת נחוש, תעזוב הכל ותשקיע 100%". "
""
"תקופה לא קצרה ניסיתי את השיטה הראשונה, אבל שום דבר לא צבר מומנטום. בסוף עבודה במשרה מלאה, משפחה, קצת תחביבים - אין באמת מקום להכל. "
""
"תקופה ארוכה שרציתי "לעשות את הצעד", אבל כל פעם משהו אחר צץ. "
"- רגע, ענבר בהיריון, לא זמן טוב לשינויים"
"- שנייה צריך להתרגל למציאות עם ילד, לא זמן טוב"
"- קידום! אתגר חדש! נחכה עוד קצת"
"-זהו דן נכנס למסגרת אחרי החגים, אבל עדיף לחכות שענבר תמצא עבודה "
""
"וככה הגענו לאוקטובר 2024. "
"עוד שנייה מתחילים לשלם על גן פרטי וענבר עמוק בחיפוש עבודה. אין זמן רע מזה, חייבים קצת יציבות. "
""
"במהלך חופשה משפחתית, נכנסו לשיחה ארוכה על הנושא. "
"בשלב מסוים ענבר אמרה לי: "אף פעם לא יהיה זמן טוב. למה שלא פשוט תנסה? נסתדר""
""
"וככה ברגע אחד, החלטנו ללכת על זה. חזרתי, נתתי 3 חודשים התראה, והתחלתי לתכנן את תקופת היזמות. ואני מ-א-ו-ד אוהב לתכנן 😅"
""
"פיצויים, דמי אבטלה, החזרי מס - בשורה התחתונה הגעתי ל-9 חודשי ראנווי. מסוף ינואר עד אחרי החגים של 2025. "
""
"המטרה שלי הייתה: להכניס את הדולר הראשון מעסק עם פוטנציאל. הנחתי שבמקרה כזה אני אוכל לגייס כסף, או ארגיש מספיק ביטחון כדי לשפוך חסכונות ולהאריך את הראנווי. "
""
"בשלושה חודשים שנותרו לי ניסיתי להתחיל קצת בערבים, אבל לא כל כך עבד לי. ענבר מצאה עבודה, דן הסתגל לגן, והזמן טס. "
""
"סוף ינואר הגיע. נפרדתי מטרניס, מקום שמאוד אהבתי ונתן לי המון. "
"ויצאתי לדרך. "
""
"---------------------"
""
"אוקיי, כמה דברים שאני לא רגיל לעשות פה:"
"א. תוכן שלא קשור לניהול צוות פיתוח 😱"
"ב. לסיים סיפור באמצע. תמיד הרגיש לי סליזי. "
"ב. עברית?! לפני שנתיים החלטתי לכתוב רק באנגלית, כדי ללכת לטווח הרחוק. וואלה השתלם רצח. "
""
"בגדול פשוט החלטתי שבא לי לכתוב גם קצת בעברית, ויותר על השנה האחרונה והמסקנות שלי ממנה. מתכנן שזה יהיה סוג של 'יומן בהמשכים' (קצת בהשראת Or Azulay). מגביל את עצמי לפעם בשבוע (ולא מתחייב להמשכיות). "
""
"אני עדיין מתכנן להמשיך באנגלית ברגע שיחזור לי זמן פנוי - דונט גט יוסד טו איט :)

The price of mandatory code reviews:"
""
"In every company I worked for in the last 15 years, we had mandatory code reviews (at least one, usually two reviewers per PR). "
""
"Over the last month, I’ve begun to reconsider this ‘law’. For the first time, I’ve met production teams that don’t have mandatory code reviews. "
""
"For example, at Pylon (YC S21) , engineers merge their own code and only request reviews if they need input, think they have a risky change, or are still onboarding. "
""
"Their thought process is: if we hire skilled engineers and trust them, there’s no reason to bottleneck every change with mandatory reviews."
""
"This actually makes a lot of sense, but I still felt uncomfortable. "
""
"So I decided to check the data (400+ companies and 3000+ engineers on Weave ) in the past month to answer 5 questions:"
""
"1. Are teams without code reviews actually faster?"
"2. Are code reviews reducing bugs?"
"3. Does the quality of code reviews matter?"
"4. Does the turnaround speed of code reviews matter?"
"5. How do top organizations work?"
""
"The TLDR:"
"You go MUCH slower with code reviews. "
"You also have MUCH fewer bugs :)"
""
"The biggest gains happen between 0 and 0.5 reviews per PR - after that, diminishing returns kick in."
""
"I think it makes sense - for some PRs it’s ok to not have mandatory code reviews. Let’s say you want to improve a log message - it’s very annoying to need to ask people to review it. "
""
"You can find the full analysis here:"
"https://lnkd.in/dh3MX3sr

I used to make fun of "boring" companies. Who wants to build another project management tool? I want to save the world!"
""
"In the last few years, I noticed that the best companies are actually exactly those boring ones."
""
"I read somewhere there's a limit to how many things you can do differently at once."
""
"If you spend your energy on creating innovative hiring and working processes, you'll probably not have enough energy for other innovations (and vice versa)."
""
"Two of my favorite companies are 37signals and Linear."
""
"The 37signals guys experiment with:"
""
"• No VC capital"
"• Profit sharing with employees"
"• Staying small"
"• Fixed (not seat-based) pricing"
"• And inventing the ShapeUp approach (which I love)"
"  "
"The Linear team:"
""
"• Started by focusing on ICs, not project managers"
"• Design and quality first"
"• Zero bug policy"
"• 10-year exercise window for stock options"
"  "
"I believe they could do it because they chose a clear market with obvious needs. They didn't try to build spaceships or create robots."
""
"They saved their "innovation budget" for what actually matters."
""
"And while I still would like to work at a 'world-changing' company someday, at this stage of my life I actually prefer a 'boring' company. "
""
"I want to build an amazing team, enjoy my day-to-day, and have some space (and freedom) for other ideas and innovation :)

The most underrated part of interviewing:"
"Reference checks. "
"You are SO close to getting an offer."
""
"You are probably competing against just 2-3 candidates now."
""
"In most cases, there is a favorite, but it might be a close call, and any advantage can count."
""
"I've done this stage tens of times from the hiring side. In 100% of the cases I got a list of names and contact details. "
""
"Nothing bad about it, aside from the fact that you are wasting a great opportunity!"
""
"In my last job search, what I did instead was to provide extra useful details."
""
"The format I used was:"
"Name:"
"Contact details:"
"Info about the person and our relationship:"
"What juicy thing do they have to say:"
""
"For example, here's the info I gave about my last manager:"
"Name: -"
"Contact details: -"
"Info: Managed me directly 3 years, first as an EM and then as a Director."
"Suggested conversation topics: What are the hard parts about managing me, and how do I deal with being told "no"."
""
"I gave 4 different people (even when 2-3 are usually required), and for each gave something 'bad' they might say about me."
""
"As a hiring manager, I would have LOVED to get such info."
""
"It shows confidence and helps to actually get to know the candidate better in that reference check."
""
"P.S.: Any creative tips for nailing that part?

Great engineering teams are super fast. People say, “It’s better to go slow in the right direction than fast in the wrong one.” I disagree:"
""
"In physics, every vector has a direction and a magnitude. 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗯𝗹𝗲𝗺 𝘄𝗶𝘁𝗵 𝘁𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗮𝗱𝘃𝗶𝗰𝗲 𝗶𝘀 𝘁𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗳𝗶𝗻𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗿𝗶𝗴𝗵𝘁 𝗱𝗶𝗿𝗲𝗰𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝘁𝗮𝗸𝗲𝘀 𝗲𝘅𝗽𝗲𝗿𝗶𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻. You need speed to experiment."
""
"Understanding the right direction takes time, and having a bigger magnitude (speed) helps you learn faster."
""
"To learn fast, you must do 2 things"
"1. Ship fast"
"2. Talk to customers a lot"
"   "
"Business outcomes come later. Teams that move twice as fast, learn twice as fast, and are then able to address their aim faster. "
""
"If it takes you two months to ship something new, your learning cycle is two months. But if you can ship something in a week, you’ll know much faster if it’s a hit or not. Is it actually working or not working?"
""
"When I joined Weave , I gained access to engineering metrics across hundreds of teams. This gave me the chance to try answering something that has always interested me - what do the most productive teams actually look like in the day-to-day?"
""
"I had a long conversation with Sumanyu (founder, CEO, and still-shipping engineer at Hamming AI) about how his 6-person team (plus 2 interns) moves faster than most 50-person engineering orgs."
""
"Here are the things that worked for him:"
"• 𝗢𝗯𝘀𝗲𝘀𝘀𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗮𝗯𝗼𝘂𝘁 𝘃𝗲𝗹𝗼𝗰𝗶𝘁𝘆 - Why learning rate dictates everything else"
"• 𝗠𝗢𝗥𝗘 𝗺𝗲𝗲𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗴𝘀 𝗳𝗼𝗿 𝗲𝗻𝗴𝗶𝗻𝗲𝗲𝗿𝘀 - Direct customer access instead of translation"
"• 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗻𝗲𝘄 𝗣𝗥 𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗰𝗲𝘀𝘀 - Architecture reviews before code, AI triage, sub-hour merges"
"• 𝗧𝗲𝘀𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗴 & 𝗾𝘂𝗮𝗹𝗶𝘁𝘆 - The next frontier: speed without regressions"
"• 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗖𝗘𝗢 𝘀𝘁𝗶𝗹𝗹 𝘀𝗵𝗶𝗽𝘀 - The “Elon algorithm” for finding real bottlenecks"
"• 𝗛𝗶𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗶𝗻𝘁𝗲𝗿𝗻𝘀 - Learning rate over accumulated knowledge"
"  "

Imagine you are tasked with building a new engineering team. Here are the requirements:"
"- You don’t know in advance what type of company/industry you’ll operate in."
"- The team should be built to work YEARS together."
"- There will be 5 engineers on your team"
""
"How will your dream team look like?"
""
"Many companies and EMs choose to go with a DPS (damage per second) team. They basically try to hire the strongest engineers they can find and squeeze the most out of them."
""
"This might be the right approach for some companies (crazy growth AI startups), but I believe that for 99% of EMs, it’s the wrong approach, even assuming you have the budget for it."
""
"Such individuals are often looking for the toughest challenges, and are very hard to keep satisfied. Look at all the chaos in OpenAI/Meta talent wars."
""
"𝗜𝗻𝘀𝘁𝗲𝗮𝗱, 𝗯𝘂𝗶𝗹𝗱 𝗮 𝗯𝗮𝗹𝗮𝗻𝗰𝗲𝗱 𝗱𝘂𝗻𝗴𝗲𝗼𝗻 𝗽𝗮𝗿𝘁𝘆"
""
"Every game has different races and classes, but it’s common to have at least 3 basic roles in a party:"
""
"- The tank - takes damage, doesn’t deal too much."
"- The DPS / damage dealer - the one actually killing the boss."
"- The healer - making sure nobody dies."
""
"There are also rogues, wizards, archers, paladins, and tons of creative things."
""
"There is a ton of research about how diverse teams are delivering more impact, are happier, and make better decisions."
""
"The best engineering teams I had the luck to work with had engineers with 𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗽𝗹𝗲𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗮𝗿𝘆 𝘀𝗸𝗶𝗹𝗹𝘀, who were not clones of each other."
""
"Going back to the initial challenge - let’s build a 5-person engineering team that will perform for years in any circumstances."
""
"This is just my personal take, nothing scientific about it! It’s supposed to be a fun exercise, and a useful model to think about your team’s composition (and where you might have gaps)."
""

I didn't do many interviews in my life, but I passed 12 of the last 13 I did. The simple trick that works even in technical interviews:"
""
"Every interview is about the same thing:"
"Would I want to work with that person?"
""
"And they are MUCH easier to pass when the interviewer has an internal "yes" after a few minutes. "
""
"You have 2 levers there: your questions, and your introduction. "
""
"For your own introduction, I really recommend not making it dry: "I worked at X, then at Y, left because of Z, I really want to work here now". "
""
"Share some things about yourself too, your hobbies, what you enjoyed in previous places. Something the other side can relate to. "
""
"As for questions:"
""
"Usually, you have some time at the end for your questions. "
""
"I try to ask if it's possible to start with those (even just 5-7 minutes). People don't object. "
""
"Ask some simple questions:"
"- What's the one thing the team is currently weakest at?"
"- What would make me fail at this job?"
""
"And then just LISTEN! "
""
"By the end of 10 minutes (intro+questions), you already showed you have some personality and that you know how to shut up and listen!"
""
"You'll see it'll influence the whole interview. "
""
"If it's a technical one, you'll get more clues."
""
"If it's a behavioral one, you'll have a more naturally flowing conversation. "
""
"The point of everything above is not to fake it - it is to be you, and let it be shown in an interview. "
""
"And last - SMILE! "
"Tom Orbach has a great tip of holding a pencil between your teeth for a few seconds. "
""
"Personally, I just try to smile for a few seconds before the conversation (and during the intros) - it gets everyone (mainly you) to the right mood .

""
"Their mistake is assuming juniors need 6-12 months to become productive, but AI-savvy juniors get up to speed much faster. They use AI to understand codebases without interrupting teammates, generate boilerplate, and just learn faster."
""

""
"Here's our 4-step hiring process:"
""
"1. Filter for the mindset - Ask about projects they've built, then drill deeper until you hit the bottom of their understanding. Look for passion and curiosity."
""
"2. "Use whatever tools you like" home assignment - Small coding challenge with explicit permission to use any tools. Then schedule a 30-minute follow-up where they walk through their solution."
""
"3. Problem solving without AI - 40-minute interview testing their ability to think through complex problems and design solutions. Focus on system design, architectural decisions, reasoning through trade-offs."
""
"4. Live implementation with AI - 20 minutes of live coding to see how they work with AI tools. Their prompting strategies, how they iterate on AI output,  can they effectively guide AI toward sensible solutions."
""

"- Talk to them! And make sure they get all the support they need."

"- Be patient with short-term productivity, but 𝗺𝗲𝗮𝘀𝘂𝗿𝗲 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗿𝗶𝗴𝗵𝘁 𝘁𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗴𝘀. Track how quickly junior engineers become productive compared to your baseline."

Engineers don’t grow by doing what they already know. They grow by working on the edge of their ability, also called the zone of proximal development:"
""
"The area in which a person can't make progress without another person with a higher skill/knowledge set to assist them (even if just a bit)."
""
"BUT, once the person has understood and completed the task, they can take on tasks of even higher difficulty, pushing their zone of proximal development toward harder and harder tasks. "
""
"When engineers are stuck for too long in the center, in the things that are known and easy, they lose the edge and stop progressing. "
""
"This means 2 things:"
""
"- Engineers need to be given tasks that sit within their zone of proximal development. "
"- Other engineers with more knowledge need to be available to help them complete those tasks."
""
"It's common knowledge that kids learn faster than adults, but if you watch any small kid - they spend lots of time attempting things that are on the edge of their ability. "
""
"How much time have you spent on the edge of your ability today?"
""
"P.S.: I read about this concept in James Stanier's "Become an Effective Software Engineering Manager" - highly reocmmended!"

The death of the hands-on engineering manager:"
""
"There are 2 types of engineering managers: Hands-on managers, and full-time managers."
""
"1. Hands-on ones take tasks in every sprint, know the codebase, and help the team fix hard bugs."
""
"2. Full-time managers attend meetings all day, have 1:1s, and have zero coding time."
""
"The sad truth is that almost all of us start from type 1, and slowly become type 2."
""
"I’ve been through that transition twice. When you are first promoted, you are close to the code. You were responsible for big parts of the codebase, and it’s easy to stay a part of the sprint."
""
"For the first couple of years, you code 30-70% of your time, with a slow decrease as your team grows."
""
"Then comes a sprint where you just have too many things. "
""
"Tons of meetings, a new project you are leading, the usual stuff. So for the first time, you decide to not take on any coding task."
""
"One sprint becomes 2, then 5, then 10."
""
"Suddenly a year went by and you barely opened your IDE. Now it’s not a question of having no time - it’s a habit you have lost. Even the most busy engineering managers can find 2-3 hours a week to do some coding."
""
"Getting back into the game is hard. You tell yourself “I can’t do anything useful in 2 hours. I’ll get into the zone and I will be distracted anyway. Better go over that document one more time”."
""
"In the article, I shared my approach to overcoming that block:"

Every EM knows this pain: you build something elegant, you’re proud of it… and then the customer just doesn’t use it the way you wanted."
""
"Florian built a 3D no-code tool, while working full-time as a Director of Engineering in a startup. Here's his story:"
""
"(For the full version, checkout the article: "
"https://lnkd.in/dmt2KZsb)"
""
"Back in 2022, there was a lot of hype in the no-code/low-code areas, but all apps felt and behaved the same. "
""
"I thought - wouldn’t it be cool if you could design your application logic not in 2D, but in 3D? Sort of like building an RTS game, but instead of armies, you’re orchestrating your app."
""
"I was putting in around two hours every evening, plus all of Sunday. By the end of the first year, I had built the whole thing - a full 3D app editor. You could create simple CRUD applications: forms that send data to a backend, store it, and retrieve it later. "
""
"Everything was fully handled by our platform - CI pipeline, Docker images, Kubernetes clusters, version control."
""
"The next step was getting customers, so we started with the obvious step: reaching out to our existing network."
""
"That’s how we found our first customer. They’re a small company, no technical people on the team, limited budget - exactly the kind of setup that could benefit from a no-code platform. They needed a tool to build internal apps, but couldn’t build it themselves."
""
"Our plan was to create a self-service product - something that semi-technical users or engineers could use themselves, through a SaaS subscription. But right from the first customer, we ran into a mismatch. "
""
"Engineers picked it up fast -they wanted more and more features like custom SQL queries and advanced logic. "
""
"But non-technical people were overwhelmed. Variables, workflows, inputs, endpoints… it was too much."
""
"Everyone loved the 3D interface - but the underlying concepts were still technical. "
""
"So in the end, we built the full application for the customer using our platform. "
""
"That was a bittersweet moment :/"
""
"The platform worked, it delivered value. The customer we built that solution for is still with us today! But we had to face the reality - we hadn’t built the kind of self-serve experience we were aiming for."
""
"-----------"
""
"Like many startups - after the bittersweet success they decided to pivot. "
""
"They kept hearing that people weren’t happy with the quality of AI-generated code, especially in industries where testing and compliance really matter. "
""
"So they decided to solve it - by helping developers write high-quality test cases - and then produce code that satisfies them. "
""
"It’s an approach that makes a lot more sense in 𝗿𝗲𝗴𝘂𝗹𝗮𝘁𝗲𝗱 𝗲𝗻𝘃𝗶𝗿𝗼𝗻𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁𝘀 like healthcare, where software bugs can have huge consequences. "
""
"This shift is already getting them some interest from developers and even VCs!"
""
"If you want to hear more - you can DM Carsten and Florian :)"

Most engineering managers wait for things to happen."

"Here are 20 simple questions to ask yourself:"
""
"1. Do you fight for your team’s raises even in tough times, or just pass down what leadership says?"
""
"2. Do you give your manager action items in your 1:1, or just answer their questions?"
""
"3. Are you actively participating in non-engineering Slack channels, or assume it’s all noise?"
""
"4. Do you chase feedback from your team and peers, or hope they’ll tell you something in the next performance review?"
""
"5. Do you have strong relationships with stakeholders in various departments, or only talk with your engineers?"
""
"6. Do you push HR for better team activity budgets, or just do the minimum?"
""
"7. Do you train your engineers to step up, or complain that you don’t have anyone you can delegate to?"
""
"8. Do you actively look for the best candidates to hire, or just look through the CVs the recruiters send you?"
""
"9. Do you push back on unrealistic deadlines early, or only speak up when things are already on fire?"
""
"10. Do you show up to leadership meetings with answers and options, or with updates and problems?"
""
"11. Are you constantly asking PMs and designers to move faster, or does everyone in the org ask you to move faster?"
""
"12. Do you go talk to customers yourself, or just read the notes your PM gives you?"
""
"13. Do you take responsibility for the business results your team delivers, or are you satisfied with a good effort?"
""
"14. Do you fully understand how the company operates and makes money or just talk about engineering velocity?"
""
"15. Do you follow up on action items after meetings, or assume someone else will?"
""
"16. Do you actively shape the roadmap or just execute what the PM tells you?"
""
"17. Do you take ownership of company-wide problems, or do you assume they belong to someone else?"

"18. Do you try hard to help your PM understand the tradeoffs, or just silently resent their decisions?"

"19. Do you think beyond just engineering and contribute to the long-term company success, or do you just focus on technical debt?"

"20. Do you share your team’s wins and progress loudly, or assume leadership will just notice?"

Most posts about Engineering Management are generic bullshit (including many of my own) because 'managing in public' is pretty much impossible:"
""
"It's not like the sexy cousin 'building in public'. When you share your struggles and milestones, people root for you, and you get customers for your app. "
""
"It's also not like an engineer who writes in public, and can share some technical struggles and successes, boosting their own visibility and also giving points to their comapny. "
""
"As a manager, our best stories and lessons involve:"
"- Our people"
"- Our managers"
"- The struggles of our company"
""
"I can't share a tough conversation I had with an employee. "
"I can't share how I fought hard for a wrong decision. "
"I can't share how I helped an engineer overcome a challenge."
"I can't share conflicts I had with my manager. "
""
"And those are the most interesting things :) "
""
"So the fallback is to either:"
"a. Write about things in the FAR past, while still changing facts. "
"Even 6 months after leaving a company, I wouldn't write about things people still there wouldn't want me to write about, or could identify themselves in the stories (or worse - others will identify them). "
""
"b. Write generic things, quotes from books, and ideas I believe in. "
""
"That's why so often I fall into option b..."
""
"If anyone has good tips on it, let me know :)

The usual hip-shoot counter to deadlines is that "fake" deadlines lead to poor work being done, and, look: there is sometimes truth to that:"
""
"---------------"
"From a great guest article by James Stanier, on how to use deadlines without driving your team crazy:"
"https://lnkd.in/dYvkwyd5"
"---------------"
""
"Such a situation typically represents poor application, rather than issues with the methodology. "
""
"Putting challenging timeboxes on projects in a healthy environment can lead to serious innovation and creativity."
""
"Doing the same with impossible timeboxes in a toxic environment will lead to all of the bad things that you expect."
""
"Deadlines really help human beings get things done. "
""
"The only way that I've written books is because I set myself a challenging, but not impossible, schedule with the publisher. "
""
"This contract of external accountability keeps the fire going through the long slog, and it forces me to make clear-cut decisions about what to include, what to leave out, and how to manage my spare time so I make progress."
""
"The exact same thing is true with communication and software projects."
""
"When you are asking people to do something, lead with a recommendation of when it should be done by. "
""
"𝗕𝗲 𝗲𝘅𝗽𝗹𝗶𝗰𝗶𝘁 𝗮𝗯𝗼𝘂𝘁 𝘁𝗵𝗶𝘀, 𝗯𝘂𝘁 𝗼𝗽𝗲𝗻 𝘁𝗼 𝗻𝗲𝗴𝗼𝘁𝗶𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻. "
""
"It's such a simple technique, but when you compound its usage over a year at a big company, you will be amazed at the difference it makes."
""
"Deadlines force a clear tempo and cadence and, fundamentally, they make things happen. "
""
"A canonical example of this is sending round a survey that can be filled in whenever versus 𝗼𝗻𝗲 𝘁𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗻𝗲𝗲𝗱𝘀 𝘁𝗼 𝗯𝗲 𝗳𝗶𝗹𝗹𝗲𝗱 𝗶𝗻 𝗯𝘆 𝘁𝗼𝗺𝗼𝗿𝗿𝗼𝘄: just by asking, you will get a much higher response rate far faster. "
""
"Learn from this, and apply it to your own communication."
""
"Even though you may not think that people want it, and even if people themselves think they don't want it, knowing that things need to be done by deadlines that are just on the cusp of the comfort zone forces real, tangible progress."
""
"If you think that a prototype might take a month, why not challenge the team to see what they can deliver by the end of the week? You will be surprised, and so will they."
""
"When wielded with grace, good intentions, and knowledge of what gets humans moving and feeling good, deadlines are a powerful tool."
""
"Parkinson's Law is real, and you will need to fight it harder the larger your organization is. "
""
"If you can succeed in this fight, you can grow and still ship fast with an org size of tens of thousands. "
""
"If you don't, then one day you'll look around and wonder why your startup turned into the software equivalent of the local council's tax office."
""
"Fight the drag. Set a deadline.

When an Engineering Manager tries to answer all his Slack messages at the end of the day:"
""
"Happy friday 🙃

EMs: Do you let the engineer1 who built app X finish the feature in 2 days, or give it to engineer2 who’ll take twice as long, just to share knowledge and mix things up?"
""
"We’ve all faced this dilemma: "
""
"In the “old” days, I really believed in the second option. My team of five had a single “team rep”:"
""
"- One engineer who was on-call for issues"
"- Also the point of contact for support"
"- Reading alert channels, triaging problems"
""
"Every engineer knew ~70-80% of the systems, and could debug reasonably well most types of problems. This had so many advantages:"
""
"- No dependency on a single person"
"- Diverse work for people"
"- Less on-call burden on the more tenured engineers"
""
"After a round of layoffs and the ‘joining’ with another team, most engineers were familiar with just 50% of the team’s responsibilities (at best)."
""
"We tried spreading the knowledge again, but "𝗘𝘃𝗲𝗿𝘆𝗼𝗻𝗲 𝗸𝗻𝗼𝘄𝘀 𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗿𝘆𝘁𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗴" 𝗯𝗲𝗰𝗮𝗺𝗲 𝘂𝗻𝘀𝘂𝘀𝘁𝗮𝗶𝗻𝗮𝗯𝗹𝗲."
""
"In the new reality, three people who know a system became a privilege, and in most cases, we had to settle for two. In the smallest systems, even just one."
""
"This is HARD on everyone. "
""
"On-call becomes harder. Instead of one person being distracted per day, every problem goes straight to the most knowledgeable person."
""
"Your job is to be aware of those new dynamics, prepare for them, and protect your people. "
""
"In every department, the reality will look different, so I don’t have a magic tip here. Talk to your manager about it, and make sure they are aware. "
""
"It’s not just about working hours, it’s also about mental exhaustion for your senior people. "
""
"What worked for me is giving people now and then ‘breather’ days, which are not deducted from their vacation days. Just a day to relax in the middle of the week.

Non-engineers often think that building a feature is the hardest part of our jobs. It’s usually not."
"The harder parts are:"
""
"- Maintaining it"
"- Supporting it"
"- Keeping it fast, secure, and relevant without creating a spaghetti codebase"
""
"Every feature you say yes to adds 𝗽𝗲𝗿𝗺𝗮𝗻𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝘄𝗲𝗶𝗴𝗵𝘁 to your team. "
""
"Even with zero tech debt (never happens), there’s a limit to how many moving parts a team can support and stay productive and sane. The more unrelated they are, the harder it is to support."
""
"So when we are asked to develop a completely new project (like adding a chat somewhere it definitely doesn’t belong), we know that we need to push back and explain why it’s a bad idea. "
""
"Still, most EMs find it VERY hard to explain this reality to business people."
""
"The ‘Multi-Dimensional Tradeoffs’ article by Will Larson can really help you here:"
"https://lnkd.in/ddscMsbC"
""
"In a nutshell:"
"1. Two-dimensional tradeoffs always disappoint someone"
"2. You can usually make a tradeoff that doesn’t disappoint anyone by introducing a new dimension"
""
"Here’s an example:"
""During annual planning, many companies struggle with intense debates about whether they invest into international expansion in new markets or do they instead prioritize their existing markets. By adding the dimension of fixed budgets, they can get varying degrees of both rather than debating existentially about doing one or the other.""
""
"Roadmap discussions are usually trapped on two axes:"
"• 𝗪𝗵𝗮𝘁 we build"
"• 𝗛𝗼𝘄 𝗹𝗼𝗻𝗴 will each item take"
""
"I’m not saying it will be easy, but if you can add the ‘Ongoing cost’ as a third dimension, it can really improve your life."
""
"A good way to start is to map the current ongoing costs of the systems you are responsible for. This is not just tech debt - it can be bug fixes, urgent customer requests that were not a part of the roadmap, outages, and so on."
""
"Let’s say you have 3 systems, and you find that ~10% of your team’s time is spent on maintaining each. So you have 70% free for ‘new’ product work."
""
"Now, you can show the estimated increase of this ongoing cost, and let 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗼𝘁𝗵𝗲𝗿 𝘀𝗶𝗱𝗲 make the decision. "
""
"Sometimes, they will still want that new feature, and that’s ok - as long as they agree to the price."
""
"Yeah, the numbers won’t be accurate, and they will fluctuate a lot, but your goal is not to be accurate, but to add the maintenance cost consideration to the table."
""
"When you start to track those things, and bring in actual stories, it makes it easier to explain your point."
""
"Here’s a great tip from Larson:"
""
""Go into each tradeoff discussion believing that there’s an additional dimension you can add that will greatly reduce the current tension in decision-making."
""
"Socialize this belief with others so they understand where you’re coming from, this can be as simple as a statement like, “I wonder if there’s a dimension we can add to this tradeoff to make it easier.”"

You see CEOs everywhere making "hard" decisions like letting people go. “Hard” in quotes because it’s not really hard for them."

90% of engineering managers fall into 1 of 4 classic traps:"
""
"- Control freaks - staying involved in every decision. "
"- People pleasers - involve the whole team every time."
"- Zero-confidence managers who depend on their boss."
"- Hands-off managers who don't care about anything. "
""
"I'm a control freak. It took me a while to understand that not everything should go through me…"
""
"Instead, we should use ALL 4 available options for decision-making:"
""
"1. 𝗦𝗼𝗹𝗼 𝗱𝗲𝗰𝗶𝘀𝗶𝗼𝗻𝗺𝗮𝗸𝗶𝗻𝗴 - 'I'll decide it myself'."
"2. 𝗣𝗮𝗿𝘁𝗶𝗰𝗶𝗽𝗮𝘁𝗶𝘃𝗲 𝗱𝗲𝗰𝗶𝘀𝗶𝗼𝗻 - getting other people involved."
"3. 𝗘𝗹𝗲𝘃𝗮𝘁𝗲𝗱 𝗱𝗲𝗰𝗶𝘀𝗶𝗼𝗻 - let your manager decide for you."

"-----------------------------------------------------"
""
""In Amazon, I learned to never use "Weasel words"."

"I stopped using terms like "significant", "low latency" or "annoying." 𝗜𝗻𝘀𝘁𝗲𝗮𝗱, 𝗜 𝘂𝘀𝗲𝗱 𝘀𝗽𝗲𝗰𝗶𝗳𝗶𝗰 𝗺𝗲𝘁𝗿𝗶𝗰𝘀, like "P99 latency jumped 375% from 12ms to 45ms." "

"I was working on 𝗺𝘆 𝗳𝗶𝗿𝘀𝘁 𝟲-𝗣𝗮𝗴𝗲𝗿, 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗶𝘁 𝘄𝗮𝘀 𝗵𝗮𝗿𝗱 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗳𝗿𝘂𝘀𝘁𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗴. "
""
"My manager connected me with a mentor who taught me how to write like an Amazonian. She taught me all about weasel words, how to identify them, and how to avoid them."
""
"But what sticks with me to this day is actually all the 1:1 meetings with my manager. "
""
"I learned that weasel words don't only appear in business documents. They also appeared in how I gave feedback to my team members. I learned to do the homework so that I could give specific feedback."
""
"I stopped saying "You are too blunt in meetings." Instead, I said "In the meeting with Christopher you cut him off when he started suggesting the React-based solution. We missed an opportunity to check more approaches. I also noticed that Christopher stopped contributing to other meetings.""

"This feedback is factual and thus indisputable. It is really so easy for the other side to understand. It is the first step to actionable advice."

9 years ago, in my first management role, I was told that my job is to be the ‘shit umbrella’ of my team:"
""
"The ‘outside world’ - customers, other teams, the management - would throw ‘shit’ at the team, and I had to protect them so they’ll be able to work in “peace and happiness”."
""
"I've stuck with this approach for a long time."
""
"I tried to make my people believe that each bad decision was the last one and that we are right on the verge of a change. When I felt the Product team was doing shitty work, I fought it myself, and presented a smiling face to the team."
""
"I didn’t share bad news and generally isolated them from the rest of the company."
""
"And while I meant well, I created a false sense of stability. "
""
"You act as a buffer to avoid discomfort. You hide chaos to keep morale up. But reality always breaks through, and when it does, your team is just not ready."
""
"In the end, this protection leads to shock, confusion, and frustration, not trust."
""
"As Ronald Heifetz and Marty Linsky wrote:"
"𝘓𝘦𝘢𝘥𝘦𝘳𝘴𝘩𝘪𝘱 𝘪𝘴 𝘥𝘪𝘴𝘢𝘱𝘱𝘰𝘪𝘯𝘵𝘪𝘯𝘨 𝘱𝘦𝘰𝘱𝘭𝘦 𝘢𝘵 𝘢 𝘳𝘢𝘵𝘦 𝘵𝘩𝘦𝘺 𝘤𝘢𝘯 𝘢𝘣𝘴𝘰𝘳𝘣"
""
"So what can you do about it?"
""
"James Stanier wrote a great article about it, titled “The disappointment frontier” (https://lnkd.in/e_Vi-jHN)"
""
"Here are some great tips he shared:"
""
"- Start with radical context-sharing. Tell your team what’s going on - even if it’s uncertain or messy. Talk about the company, other teams, customers, shifting priorities. Let them see the full picture."
""
"- Be honest about control. What decisions are yours? Own them - even if it makes you the “bad guy.” It’s better than hiding behind “leadership decided…” That just builds resentment."
""
"- For things you can’t control, be a collaborator.  "
"Example: You don’t control promotions, but you can help prepare a great packet, coach them, and vouch for them in the room. If the answer is no, you’re still on their side. You can continue this collaboration to help them understand why and what they can do to increase their chances next time around."
""
"Ask yourself:"
"• What are you shielding your team from right now?"
"• Are you overpromising anything?"
"• Where can you be more transparent?"
"• Are you pretending not to be the decision maker to avoid conflict?"
""
"Our job is not to build a utopia, but to help our team succeed in the messy reality.

During her time as an EM in Google Compute Engine, Chaitali had to make a big headcount request for a new project:"
""
""We needed a new pre-prod environment that would allow us to test various complex scenarios for the product with efficiency and simplicity. This was a big task, the Compute stack was huge. "
""
"It included different types of physical machines in a data center, networking layers, and the hypervisor."
""
"The pre-prod environment had endless use cases. Some teams wanted to test disaster recovery. Others aimed to check the UI layer’s integration. Some also wanted to test specific applications, like SAP, that our customers often used on the VMs."
""
"My approach in such cases is to use the "Decoy Pricing Method". "
""
"Decoy Pricing is a popular marketing strategy. It encourages customers to choose a specific product. Here is an example:"
""
"Suppose you go to a cafe and see that a small cup costs $2.99 and a large cup is $7.99. The price difference seems large, so you are most likely to pick the small cup. If the cafe offers a medium cup for $6.99, you might choose the large cup instead. "
""
"The difference is only a dollar, so it seems like a better deal. Here, the medium cup is the decoy."
""
"When requesting the headcount, I used the decoy pricing method to build the proposal: I provided three options for different headcount increases: Small, Medium, and Large. "
""
"For each request, I outlined what we would create and how the system would work in terms of Speed, Ease, and Quality."
""
" I felt a sense of joy when our leadership chose the Large option, just as Decoy Pricing predicted!"
""
"This method also works when you want the decider to pick the smallest option - when asking a peer team's manager for help with our launch, I would present three options. "
""
"The smallest option would be the work I truly need from them!""
""

Chaitali joined Google as an intern. In 10 years, she was promoted 5 times- to Director. Her secret? A simple Japanese approach called 𝘕𝘦𝘮𝘢𝘸𝘢𝘴𝘩𝘪:"
""
"It was one of five powerful persuasion methods for engineering managers she shared in her guest article for manager.dev:"
"https://lnkd.in/dzBXb3nm"
""
"Nemawashi is a Japanese term that translates to “going around the roots.” It's a Japanese gardening technique used when transplanting trees."
""
"Transplantation (moving the tree from one location to another) is a large change for a big tree. "
""
"Gently digging at its roots, one strand at a time, helps prepare the tree for the shock of the big change."
""
"As a manager, you can use the Nemawashi method when you are persuading a lot of stakeholders. The best example of this is if you want to get a higher calibration rating for your report."
""
"At Google, only a small percentage of candidates receive the “Outstanding” rating. This applies to 18% of those in organizations with 200 or more members. "
""
"So only 36 people across all levels would get this rating in a director-sized organization."
""
"In one calibration cycle, I had a report who received a promotion 6 months ago. By the general rule, they would receive a “Significant Impact” rating. This is Google’s version of “Meets Expectations.” "
""
"I felt they exceeded expectations even after the promotion and deserved the “Outstanding” rating. "
""
"The 𝗡𝗲𝗺𝗮𝘄𝗮𝘀𝗵𝗶 method helped me convince most managers in my director's organization, including my director, of this."
""
"I met 1:1 with a few peer managers. "
""
"I chose those who had experience and influence in the organization. This was before the main calibration meeting."
""
"In each 1:1, I 𝘁𝗮𝗶𝗹𝗼𝗿𝗲𝗱 𝗺𝘆 𝗿𝗲𝗮𝘀𝗼𝗻𝘀 for why my report deserved an Outstanding rating to the individual I was meeting. "
""
"Some valued technical excellence, so I showed them well-written design docs. Others valued high throughput, so I showed them my report’s large number of coding contributions. Some others looked at business impact. So, I shared peer reviews from cross-functional partners, like design, PM, and GTM, who had worked with my report."
""
"With Nemawashi, I gained time to hear their honest feedback. I listened to their objections and addressed their concerns. This helped me win their support."
""
"Getting this support ahead of time made the actual calibrations much less contentious. This is how I was able to avoid the calibration blindside for my top performer and get them the Outstanding rating they deserved."
""

The hardest part about swimming in the open sea isn’t the waves, the cold, or even the distance. It’s swimming in the right direction 😅"
""
"In a triathlon, it happens all the time - people need to swim 1,500 meters but end up doing 2K, sometimes even 2.5K. Some even get disqualified."
""
"Why? Because you drift."
""
"Let’s say you aim for point A. You raise your head, set your direction, and start swimming."
""
"But there’s no black line like in the pool. Winds push you. Waves shift you. And you don’t notice - until you stop, look up, and realize how far off course you are..."
""
"So you correct."
""
"But if you wait too long, you waste tons of energy. "
"Look up too often, and you crawl forward."

At my first meeting with the CEO, everyone talked about Gross Margins and GRR. I didn’t understand shit. So I decided that wouldn’t happen again:"
""
"If you want real influence - with product, finance, or execs - you need to learn their language."
""
"I broke down 20 business terms every EM should know, with real examples from my own career. No fluff. No MBA required. "

""
"Here are 10 that actually changed how I operate:"
""
" 1. 𝗕𝘂𝗿𝗻 𝗥𝗮𝘁𝗲 - How much cash the company 'loses' monthly. Burn $200K/month with $1M in the bank = 5 months left."
"    "
" 2. 𝗗𝗲𝗳𝗮𝘂𝗹𝘁 𝗔𝗹𝗶𝘃𝗲/𝗗𝗲𝗮𝗱 - Will you hit profitability before the money runs out (assuming no new funds and same growth)?"
"    "
" 3. 𝗖𝗢𝗚𝗦 - Costs that scale with users: storage, support, per-seat licenses."
"    "
" 4. 𝗚𝗿𝗼𝘀𝘀 𝗠𝗮𝗿𝗴𝗶𝗻 - What’s left after COGS. High GM = efficient delivery. SaaS is ~80%, hardware is much lower."
"    "
" 5. 𝗡𝗲𝘁 𝗠𝗮𝗿𝗴𝗶𝗻 - What's left after all costs - COGS and OpEx. Shows the financial health of the company."
"    "
" 6. 𝗢𝗽𝗘𝘅 - Day-to-day expenses like salaries and marketing. Cut OpEx and your product still runs, just slower."
"    "
" 7. 𝗔𝗥𝗥 - Annual Recurring Revenue. $100/month = $1,200 ARR. "
"    "
" 8. 𝗖𝗔𝗖 - Customer Acquisition Cost. Spend $5K and get 50 users? CAC = $100."
"    "
" 9. 𝗟𝗧𝗩 - Lifetime Value. How much a customer brings in over time. Helps justify CAC."
"    "
"10. 𝗠𝗼𝗮𝘁 - What makes your product hard to copy or compete with. Brand, tech, scale, network effects."
"    "

Most engineering "culture" problems fall into 1 of 3 types:"
"- You and your manager VS the team"
"- Your team and your manager VS you"
"- You and your team VS your manager"
""
"When everything works perfectly, you have alignment:"
"- You, your team, and your manager all see the same truth"
"- Problems get solved quickly"
"- Everyone agrees on what needs fixing"
""
"Example: Your production incidents are too high, but since everyone sees it, fixing it becomes the top priority."
""
"But perfect alignment is rare."
""
"There 3 blind spots in every engineering team:"
""
"1. Poor Downward Communication "
""
"You and your manager see the problem, but your team doesn't."
""
"Example:"
"Your production bugs are higher than the average team."
"But your team doesn't see it as an issue."
""
"2. Poor Upward Communication "
""
"You see the problem this time."
"So does your team."
""
"It's just the manager that isn't aligned."
""
"Example:"
"Your team is drowning in a roadmap that isn't achievable."
"But your manager still sees the expected progress."
""
"This is where burnout begins to happen."
""
"3. False Beliefs "
""
"It's when you notice issues, but both the team and manager see it as normal."
""
"Example:"
"Your team is used to monthly deployments with manual QA."
"They see it as "just how it works.""
""
"But you've witnessed other teams delivering daily."
"They have automated testing."
""
"This mainly happens when you experience a better benchmark, and it can be hard to change false beliefs."
""
"Here's how you can make use of this framework:"
""
"1. Map your biggest challenges into these intersections."
"2. Start with any communication gaps."
"3. Then tackle the false beliefs."
"4. Build towards alignment."
""
"The reality is that most engineering problems aren't technical..."
""
"They sit in these intersections.
