Engineering management is an impossible balancing act:"
""
"1. You need to be humble, but not passive."
"   "
"2. You need to talk enough that your team knows where you stand, but not too much otherwise they’ll stop paying attention."
"   "
"3. You need to rely on metrics to make decisions, but also pay attention to the hearts and minds of employees and customers."
"   "
"And so on."
""
"If you are blind to that balancing act, you’ll lean too far in one direction. "
""
"Sometimes, your biggest advantages can bring you down:"
""
"• 𝗔𝗿𝗲 𝘆𝗼𝘂 𝗮 𝗴𝗿𝗲𝗮𝘁 𝗰𝗼𝗱𝗲𝗿? Then you are probably too involved in the technical details, having a hard time delegating decisions to your developers."
""
"• 𝗔𝗿𝗲 𝘆𝗼𝘂 𝗴𝗼𝗼𝗱 𝗮𝘁 𝘁𝗮𝗸𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗼𝘄𝗻𝗲𝗿𝘀𝗵𝗶𝗽 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗰𝗼𝘃𝗲𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗳𝗼𝗿 𝘆𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝗽𝗲𝗼𝗽𝗹𝗲? Your team members probably have nothing left for which they can take ownership… They execute only at your specific direction, which results in a far less capable team."
""
"• 𝗔𝗿𝗲 𝘆𝗼𝘂 𝗰𝗹𝗼𝘀𝗲 𝘁𝗼 𝘆𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝗽𝗲𝗼𝗽𝗹𝗲? Then you probably avoid some difficult conversations, and will find it very hard to lay off people when the time comes."
""
"The list of dichotomies is endless - each leader has their own balancing act to deal with."
""
"Reading 'The Dichotomy of Leadership' (by Willink and Babin, authors of Extreme Ownership) really opened my mind about this one.

Have you ever seen a roadmap like this:"
"October:      Feature Y Phase 1"
"November:  Feature Y Phase 2"
"December:   Feature Y Phase 3"
""
"This is the most common interpretation of having an ‘agile’, ‘lean’, ‘startup-like’ (pick your favorite buzzword) mentality."
""
"On the surface, it sounds legit. Instead of releasing a huge feature all at once, you break it down into smaller parts. You understand what is the minimum viable product, and what can be done later."
""
"That's a problem."
""
"If you decide in advance how that feature will be developed, you will be forever biased to follow that roadmap."
""
"So when you get some customer feedback, 𝘆𝗼𝘂 𝗵𝗲𝗮𝗿 𝘄𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝘆𝗼𝘂 𝘄𝗮𝗻𝘁 𝘁𝗼 𝗵𝗲𝗮𝗿. 𝗔𝗻𝘆 𝗻𝗲𝗴𝗮𝘁𝗶𝘃𝗲 𝘀𝗶𝗴𝗻𝗮𝗹 𝗮𝗯𝗼𝘂𝘁 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗳𝘂𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲 𝗽𝗵𝗮𝘀𝗲𝘀 𝘄𝗶𝗹𝗹 𝗯𝗲 𝗶𝗴𝗻𝗼𝗿𝗲𝗱, 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗽𝗼𝘀𝗶𝘁𝗶𝘃𝗲 𝗼𝗻𝗲𝘀 𝘄𝗶𝗹𝗹 𝗯𝗲 𝗰𝗹𝘂𝗻𝗴 𝘁𝗼."
""
"When you have a well-defined ‘phase 2’ in the roadmap, it means that you already decided. "
""
"The chances you will stop after phase 1 are very small. "
""
"The same problem exists with MVPs and PoCs - if a ‘PoC with a first customer’ is followed in the roadmap by ‘Feature Y improvements’, you fall into the same trap."
""
"The point of being agile, lean, fast - is not only to release in smaller chunks. It is also to be able to change your mind."
""
"When you are consulted about a roadmap for your team, ask those questions, from the easiest to the hardest:"
""
"1. How are we going to 𝗺𝗲𝗮𝘀𝘂𝗿𝗲 this feature?"
"2. How is the 𝘀𝘂𝗰𝗰𝗲𝘀𝘀 of phase 1 / MVP / PoC defined?"
"3. What will happen if the criteria are 𝗻𝗼𝘁 𝗺𝗲𝘁?"
"4. How will the 𝘀𝗰𝗼𝗽𝗲 𝗳𝗼𝗿 𝗽𝗵𝗮𝘀𝗲 𝟮 be decided?"
"5. What can make us decide to 𝗡𝗢𝗧 𝗰𝗼𝗻𝘁𝗶𝗻𝘂𝗲 with this feature?"
""

Most Engineering Managers make 3 incredibly annoying mistakes when it comes to deadlines:"
""
"1. 𝗡𝗼𝘁 𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗺𝘂𝗻𝗶𝗰𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗴 to the team what will happen after a deadline"
""
"It’s completely ok to not have anything external happen when the deadline arrives."
""
"That doesn’t mean the effort to meet it was wasted. You built trust with the rest of the organization and you freed yourself to work on other things."
""
"BUT, if you don't set the expectations in advance, people might work really hard to meet a 'fake' deadline, and will be left disappointed when nothing happens afterward (the feature is not released to users, for example)."
""
"2. 𝗡𝗼𝘁 𝗰𝗼𝗻𝘀𝘂𝗹𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗴 with your team about what’s reasonable"
""
"Deadlines work best when the team takes part in deciding the scope that can be achieved. It doesn’t mean you need to reach a consensus, it’s ok to take a different decision - as long as they were part of the conversation."
""
"Imagine the frustration of starting on a project you know from day 1 you won’t be able to achieve on time. If your engineers think “If only somebody asked me…” - you failed."
""
"3. 𝗣𝘂𝘀𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗴 your team too hard to meet a deadline"
""
"The whole point of the deadline is to get the team focused on the goal. Even if you all agree together about the achievable scope, things change."
""
"Maybe you encountered an unexpected obstacle, or were blocked by another team for a week. Or maybe it just took more than you anticipated."
""
"Being very strict with the rules, and taking pride in the fact that ‘my team always delivers on time!’ can create unnecessary conflicts. "
""
"It’s probably better to miss a fake deadline once in a while than make your team work extra hours and weekends."

7 game-changing mental models for engineering managers:"
""
"𝟭. 𝗜𝗻𝘃𝗲𝗿𝘀𝗶𝗼𝗻"
""
"Invert the problem to look at the opposite perspective. List all the things that should be avoided."
""
"For example: Instead of thinking about how to cooperate with other teams better, focus on things that should be done to make it worse (and then invert)."
""
"𝟮. 𝗜𝗻𝘁𝗲𝗿𝗶𝗮"
""
"Your tendency to resist changes and keep doing things in the same way. Think where it hurts you"
""
"For example:"
"You don’t have team meetings because you just never did."
""
"You don’t try to play with AI tools because you like your old code editor."
""
"𝟯. 𝗦𝗲𝗰𝗼𝗻𝗱-𝗼𝗿𝗱𝗲𝗿 𝗧𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗸𝗶𝗻𝗴"
""
"Consider more than just one immediate result of your decisions.  "
""
"Ask “And then what?”"
""
"For example: You need to push feature for a demo. and decide to do it fast, without tests:"
"First-order consequences: it works during the demo."
"Second-order consequences: different parts of the app break and the demo is ruined."
""
"𝟰. 𝗘𝗻𝘁𝗿𝗼𝗽𝘆"
""
"Every system you have will eventually degrade if you don’t take action."
""
"For example: "
"-Your codebase is going to get worse and worse without proper maintenance, as its complexity increases."
""
"-Your team trained in security measures (or any kind of best practices). 6-months later, security is all but ignored. "
""
"𝟱. 𝗛𝗮𝗻𝗹𝗼𝗻’𝘀 𝗥𝗮𝘇𝗼𝗿"
""
"“Never attribute to malice that which is adequately explained by stupidity.” - In other words, assume good intentions."
""
"For example: The business team promised a feature that is impossible to deliver - do you assume they want you to fail? Or maybe they just don’t understand the app?"
""
""
"𝟲. 𝗕𝗼𝘁𝘁𝗹𝗲𝗻𝗲𝗰𝗸𝘀"
""
"Where does it all fail? Usually in the most vulnerable part - the bottleneck."
""
"For example:"
"-There might be one great engineer who holds all the knowledge - if they leave, it could significantly slow down your work."
""
"-You might be a bottleneck - your capacity is limited, and if everything in your team goes through you, you become the limiting factor."
""
"𝟳. 𝗔𝗰𝘁𝗶𝘃𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗘𝗻𝗲𝗿𝗴𝘆"
""
"Activation energy is a concept drawn from chemistry, where a certain amount of energy is needed to start a reaction."
""
"For example: "
"-The onboarding process is costly and takes a lot of time to get new engineers up to speed. When activation energy is high, people are reluctant to hire more."
" "
"To help with this, make sure to improve the process incrementally with each new engineer on-boarded."
""

If you start a job feeling underpaid, it's almost impossible to recover from it. The approach 'I will show them what I am worth, and they'll pay me accordingly' just doesn't work."
""
"In most companies, you will get a  5-7% raise (best case!) every year, no matter how good you are."
""
"So what can you do? Negotiate for what you are worth 𝗯𝗲𝗳𝗼𝗿𝗲 starting the job. "
""
"4 great tips from an article I've recently read (by Josh Doody and Ben Lang):"
"https://lnkd.in/eJjcnu2m"
""
"𝟭. 𝗡𝗲𝗴𝗼𝘁𝗶𝗮𝘁𝗲 𝗼𝘃𝗲𝗿 𝗲𝗺𝗮𝗶𝗹 𝗮𝘀 𝗹𝗼𝗻𝗴 𝗮𝘀 𝗽𝗼𝘀𝘀𝗶𝗯𝗹𝗲"
""
"The recruiter on the other side is a professional. They negotiate every week more offers than you negotiated in your life."
""
"Negotiating in writing gives you the chance to write your argument in your own words, and not rely on the recruiter to summarize it for the other people in the approval chain."
""
"𝟮. 𝗔𝘀𝗸 𝗳𝗼𝗿 𝗼𝗻𝗲 𝘁𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗮𝘁 𝗮 𝘁𝗶𝗺𝗲, 𝘀𝘁𝗮𝗿𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘄𝗶𝘁𝗵 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝘁𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘆𝗼𝘂 𝗰𝗮𝗿𝗲 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗺𝗼𝘀𝘁 𝗮𝗯𝗼𝘂𝘁. "
""
"Don't ask lots of things to try to get the most stuff possible. This just gives your recruiter a list of things to choose from when making concessions, and they’re almost always going to choose the cheapest things"
""
"𝟯. 𝗗𝗼𝗻'𝘁 𝘀𝗵𝗮𝗿𝗲 𝘀𝗮𝗹𝗮𝗿𝘆 𝗲𝘅𝗽𝗲𝗰𝘁𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀 𝘂𝗽𝗳𝗿𝗼𝗻𝘁"
""
"If they pressure you, just say:"
""I don’t really have a number in mind right now. I’m excited for this opportunity and I know I’ll be a valuable addition to the team. I look forward to hearing what you suggest in terms of comp when we get to that stage.”"
""
"For a deeper dive into this tricky one, Josh wrote an additional article:"
"https://lnkd.in/erdnXfNw"
""
"𝟰. 𝗔𝘀𝗸 𝗳𝗼𝗿 𝗮 𝗺𝗲𝗮𝗻𝗶𝗻𝗴𝗳𝘂𝗹 𝗶𝗺𝗽𝗿𝗼𝘃𝗲𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗼𝗳 𝟭𝟬-𝟮𝟬% 𝗺𝗼𝗿𝗲 𝘁𝗵𝗮𝗻 𝘁𝗵𝗲𝗶𝗿 𝗶𝗻𝗶𝘁𝗶𝗮𝗹 𝗼𝗳𝗳𝗲𝗿"
""
"Our tendency is to ask for less than we should."
""
"Even if you really want that job, an offer will never be rescinded just because you negotiate. "
""
"By extending the offer, they’re saying they 𝘄𝗮𝗻𝘁 𝘆𝗼𝘂 to do the job. "
""
"They spent a lot of time and money to identify, recruit, interview, vet, and offer each candidate they hire. They’re unlikely to just waste all of that time and money in response to a counteroffer when they could also just say, “Appreciate that you’re asking for more comp, but we’ve made our best offer and we hope you’ll accept it.”"

The 5 most common excuses engineering managers use for not doing 1:1 meetings with their developers:"
""
"1. We sit in the same room, we talk all the time."
"2. We talk informally, I know what's going on."
"3. The developers don't want it, I asked."
"4. They have too many meetings."
"5. I'm not their mother."
""
"But if you try to dive deeper, you'll figure out the actual reasons are:"
"• They are worried about what developers will think."
"• They think they'll have nothing to talk about."
"• They are introverts and don't like talking."
"• It is not common in their organization."
""
"I've been in that place."
""
"In my first management role, I didn't do any(!) 1:1s."
""
"Nobody around me did 🤷‍♂️"
""
"In my next role, I was afraid of them."
""
"I had to collect myself before each meeting."
"I was constantly worried about how it would go."
""
"And it was HARD."
""
"Some 1:1s lasted for 5-10 minutes."
"Some I canceled any time I could."
"Some were only about the sprint."
""
"It took some time, but after a few months they became my favorite (and most informative) meetings of the week."
""
"----------------------------------------------------"
""
"6 tips that worked for me:"
""
"1. Don't be afraid of 𝗳𝗶𝗻𝗶𝘀𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗲𝗮𝗿𝗹𝘆. "
"Even 10-15 minutes can be meaningful, if you make it so."
""
"2. Have a 𝘄𝗲𝗲𝗸𝗹𝘆 𝗺𝗲𝗲𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗴 in the calendar. "
"Even 30 minutes is good. Some people think it's too much. I strongly disagree. You'll see the effects, try it. "
""
"3. 𝗗𝗼𝗻'𝘁 𝗰𝗮𝗻𝗰𝗲𝗹 a meeting due to stupid reasons."
"It's the last meeting that should be canceled. Unless you are on vacation, or it's a total disaster - don't skip it. "
""
"4. 𝗦𝗵𝗮𝗿𝗲 𝗮𝗯𝗼𝘂𝘁 𝘆𝗼𝘂𝗿𝘀𝗲𝗹𝗳 - personal life, struggles."
"If you want to get to know the other side, you have to open up. This builds trust over time. "
""
"5. Don't fall into the '𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗿𝘆𝗱𝗮𝘆' 𝘁𝗿𝗮𝗽."
"It can be tempting to use the time to talk only about work stuff. While it's ok to use it like that, make sure to at least start with a personal conversation."
""
"6. Talk once in a while about 𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗳𝗲𝘀𝘀𝗶𝗼𝗻𝗮𝗹 𝗴𝗼𝗮𝗹𝘀."
"It'll help you understand if the day-to-day tasks are aligned with the person's goals. If it's easy/boring for too long - you have to make some adjustments."
""
""
"Take your 1:1s seriously. "
"You'll see the results.

I was waiting for a bus with a coworker. 2 buses arrived, numbers 40 and 240. He said “Let’s get number 40, that’s my usual one”, but I insisted: “240 will get us there faster”."
""
"He listened to me. Turns out 240 was not even in the right direction..."
""
"I laughed about it, but he said seriously: “You know Anton, you do that A LOT. You are very sure about everything. Most of the time you are right, but in the 10% you are wrong, it’s very annoying. If you had given me even a little clue you are not 100% sure, I would have insisted on my opinion”."
""
"Using inaccurate language can really harm your career."
""
"Some people are overly confident when they speak. They make big statements but don’t have evidence to back up their claims."
""
"Others caveat everything, and add statements like “but you don’t have to listen to me, you can do whatever you want,” on areas where they have expertise."
""
"I was in the first camp, and since that incident, I tried hard to re-evaluate my confidence in things before I speak, and put proper caveats. "
""
"If you are in the second camp, maybe you need a bit more of the first :)"
"

The days when engineering managers had no say in what to build are over:"
" "

A critical deadline arrives in a week and you figure out that your team won't make it. What do you do?"
""
"Here's my take. "
""
"Let's start with what almost never works:"
""
"- 𝗔𝗱𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗽𝗲𝗼𝗽𝗹𝗲"
""
"You will be even later. More people, more communication (and miscommunication)."
""
"The only way that worked for me was adding senior developers as 'shadows', being always available to bounce ideas and code together with the current developers (basically pair programming)."
""
"- '𝗥𝗲𝗱𝘂𝗰𝗶𝗻𝗴' 𝗾𝘂𝗮𝗹𝗶𝘁𝘆"
""
"Deciding to 'do the tests later', or 'only do a basic QA'."
""
"Whatever you will gain by being on time, you will lose in the long term because of those decisions. "
""
""
"Next let's move to what works sometimes:"
""
"- 𝗔𝗱𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘄𝗼𝗿𝗸 𝘁𝗶𝗺𝗲"
""
"Requesting the team to work additional hours, in the evenings and/or weekends. "
""
"I think there IS a place for this in some cases. The interest of everyone is for the company to succeed, and if you align expectations beforehand (and compensate accordingly), it's an option you shouldn't be afraid to use. "
""
"Of course, then senior leaders will expect you to use it all the time, and you'll have to know where to draw the line."
""
"And last, what worked the best for me:"
""
"- 𝗥𝗲𝗱𝘂𝗰𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘀𝗰𝗼𝗽𝗲"
""

Being explicit is NOT micromanagement. 99% of managers are not explicit enough, especially with juniors:"
""
"They throw their engineers into the deep water, and then feel resentful that they don't rise to the opportunity. "
""
"Think about situations like:"
"• Code reviews"
"• On-call responsibilities"
"• Writing documentation"
"• Creating a design for a new feature"
""
"For a senior engineer, all of them are obvious and not challenging at all. "
""
"But here’s the thing - we often forget what it’s like to be in the shoes of someone doing it for the first time."
""
"Think back: do you remember the stress of your first on-call shift, constantly worried you’d miss something critical? "
""
"Or the uncertainty of requesting your first code review in a giant, unfamiliar codebase? "
""
"Or the terror of presenting your design to a room full of much more senior engineers?"
""
"𝗟𝗲𝘁’𝘀 𝘁𝗮𝗸𝗲 𝗮 𝗱𝗲𝘀𝗶𝗴𝗻 𝗿𝗲𝘃𝗶𝗲𝘄 𝗮𝘀 𝗮𝗻 𝗲𝘅𝗮𝗺𝗽𝗹𝗲. "
""
"You have a junior engineer who is tasked with designing a new feature and presenting it in a design review meeting."
""
"Here’s what you can say to help them:"
""
"“Don’t worry if it feels huge, I’ve been there too. Let’s break it down together."
""
"A useful way to think about this is that you’re the conductor and your colleagues are the orchestra. You will be creating the melody (the design), and the meeting is like the final rehearsal to ensure everyone’s aligned and ready to play their part."
""
"It’s your job to ensure all the prep is done 𝗯𝗲𝗳𝗼𝗿𝗲𝗵𝗮𝗻𝗱 so the meeting runs smoothly - it isn’t the time to rewrite the music."
""
"Here’s what I’d recommend:"
""
"- Share early and often: Instead of creating the design in isolation and only asking for feedback once it’s finished, share a rough skeleton early on and involve others to refine it. You might have to completely redo it - it’s ok!"
""
"- I’m here to help: Share drafts with me as soon as you need feedback - I’ll make sure to respond quickly and support you throughout the process."
""
"- Pre-align with stakeholders: Everyone attending the meeting should already be familiar with the design. Sending them a link is not enough, use 1:1s if you need to walk them through it beforehand. Hear their thoughts, and address concerns early."
""
"- Iterate as needed: For me, it typically took 2-3 iterations to refine a design. Plan for this upfront to avoid last-minute rushing."
""
"- Schedule a dry run: Please schedule a dry run with me at least a day before the design review. We’ll walk through the design together, simulate the meeting, and ensure you’re fully prepared."
""
"- Guide the meeting: You’re responsible for leading the discussion during the meeting. There should be no surprises. Walk everyone through the design, clarify trade-offs, and align on the next steps."
""
"These are just a few tips off the top of my head, but there might be other aspects to consider based on the situation.”"
""

If you rejected a candidate because of a reason that might get you sued - you have a bigger problem. Think creatively, maybe the latest interviewer can provide written feedback that will be passed to the candidate after the rejection call?"

5 reasons why managing a Platform/DevOps team is so damn hard:"
""
"1. 𝗣𝗿𝗶𝗼𝗿𝗶𝘁𝗶𝗲𝘀 𝗮𝗿𝗲 𝗰𝗵𝗮𝗻𝗴𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗳𝗮𝘀𝘁𝗲𝗿 𝘁𝗵𝗮𝗻 𝗮 𝗝𝗲𝗻𝗸𝗶𝗻𝘀 𝗯𝘂𝗶𝗹𝗱 🌪️"
""
"Forget about quiet sprints, with high velocity and zero interruptions. Something is ALWAYS urgent in the DevOps/Platform world. "
""
"2. 𝗛𝗶𝗴𝗵 𝗲𝘅𝗽𝗲𝗰𝘁𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗱𝗲𝗺𝗮𝗻𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗰𝗹𝗶𝗲𝗻𝘁𝘀 🏔️"
""
"Your ‘clients’ are super-technical, and most of the time think they know better than you what you should be working on. This leads to the feeling that you always under-deliver. "
""
"3. 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗰𝗼𝗻𝗻𝗲𝗰𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝘁𝗼 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗯𝘂𝘀𝗶𝗻𝗲𝘀𝘀 💼"
""
"This one is hard even for a user-facing team, but for a developer-facing one…  Your work doesn’t contribute directly to the company’s bottom line, or help the customers. "
""
"You feel disconnected from the business, sometimes not even knowing how exactly is the product used. When people feel they don’t have a clear purpose - it has a big effect on their motivation."
""
"4. 𝗩𝗶𝘀𝗶𝗯𝗶𝗹𝗶𝘁𝘆 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗿𝗲𝗰𝗼𝗴𝗻𝗶𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 🏆"
""
"When things break - it’s always the ‘Platform team’s fault!’. "
""
"When a new feature is delivered to the customers - it’s never thanks to the DevOps team. "
""
"Working behind the scenes can be a thankless job. "
""
"5. 𝗥𝗲𝗹𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀𝗵𝗶𝗽 𝘄𝗶𝘁𝗵 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗣𝗿𝗼𝗱𝘂𝗰𝘁 𝗠𝗮𝗻𝗮𝗴𝗲𝗿 (𝗣𝗠) 🤝"
""
"When the product the team works on does not have a Figma, it makes the PM’s job much harder. Especially if the PM is not technical - this can be a disaster."
""

If your developer asks you for a raise, you already lost. Only 3 things can happen:"
""
"1. You don't have the budget for it. They'll be disappointed and probably leave soon."
""
"2. You give them a raise, but less than what they asked for. They'll be disappointed. "
""
"3. You give them the raise, as you planned to do anyway. They'll feel they got what they deserve, and you get no bonus points (even if you had to fight for it). "
""
"In any case of the above, you already 𝗳𝗮𝗶𝗹𝗲𝗱."
""
"There is just no way you can get out of it in a good way. "
""
"Instead, 𝗬𝗢𝗨 should be the one taking the initiative."
""
"Let's flip the scenarios. "
""
"1. No raise: You tell them you really appreciate the work they do (if you really do), but there will be no raises this year due to the business situation. They might still be angry, but there is a chance they'll understand. "
""
"2. Small raise: You tell them they'll get a raise. It's less than they expected, so they might ask for an additional one, but you'll get some bonus points. "
""
"3. Good raise: You tell them they'll get the raise they expected, and they'll appreciate not needing to ask."
""
"It's actually not that hard to do. If your regular 'raises season' is in December, ask your manager to reach a decision a few weeks earlier, and initiate that conversation.

One of your engineers tells you they signed with another company, and will leave in 30 days. What do you do?"
""
"If you haven't experienced that feeling - it really hurts. "
""
"You immediately start to blame yourself. "
""
""People leave managers not jobs", right?"
""
"And being surprised feels awful. Shouldn't you have seen it coming?"
""
"My answer would be to wish them good luck. "
""
"Then, do a retrospective with yourself. Try to understand what you could have done differently."
""
"Then, a few days later (after calming down) - talk to them, and understand if your assumption was true, or if it was something completely different that made them leave. "
""
"Yes, we should learn from our mistakes. "
""
"But we can't take it personally. Your focus should be on your current employees, and on the future.

"Here is a quote I absolutely love from ‘Unreasonable Hospitality', a book about how Will Guidara created the best restaurant in the world:"

"-----------------------------------------------"
""
""In fine dining, there’s often a divide between those who cook and those who serve, with chefs usually winning the tug-of-war. "
""
"I needed to know if Daniel [the chef he wanted to work with] would approach our partnership differently."
""
"“I love hospitality,” I told him. “I want to make people happy. I don’t want to spend my life convincing you that what I do is as important as what you do. If the dining room doesn’t matter as much as the kitchen, I don’t want to start.”"
""
"Daniel understood. He’d worked at a European restaurant where the culinary team wasn’t allowed in the dining room, and another where the 𝘀𝗲𝗿𝘃𝗶𝗰𝗲 𝘁𝗲𝗮𝗺 𝗵𝗮𝗱 𝘁𝗼 𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗺𝘂𝗻𝗶𝗰𝗮𝘁𝗲 𝘄𝗶𝘁𝗵 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗸𝗶𝘁𝗰𝗵𝗲𝗻 𝘁𝗵𝗿𝗼𝘂𝗴𝗵 𝗻𝗼𝘁𝗲𝘀 𝗽𝗮𝘀𝘀𝗲𝗱 𝘂𝗻𝗱𝗲𝗿 𝗮 𝗽𝗹𝗲𝘅𝗶𝗴𝗹𝗮𝘀𝘀 𝘄𝗶𝗻𝗱𝗼𝘄. "

One of your developers is constantly late to meetings. You've talked to them about it, and it didn't help. What do you do?"
""
"Early in my management career, I used to be super strict about everyone being on time."
""
"I took it personally when people were late to my meetings. "
""
"Then I met my wife, who taught me not everyone's brains work the same 😂"
""
"For some people, it's easy. They are ordered and organized, and they live by their schedule."
""
"Other people are more chaotic, but they don't do it on purpose. Even when they try hard, with alarms and nofitications, they will still be late now and then. "
""
"I've learned that the less things I'm strict about, the easier is the life of people."
""
"So what if someone is late to the daily standup by 2-3 minutes?"
""
"On the other hand, if multiple people will be late it'll waste everyone's time. "

If your PM is not technical enough, it's YOUR job to help them become so:"
""
"If they don't have a technical background, it doesn't mean they can't learn. "
""
"Spend the time to explain the benefits of ‘purely technical’ tasks."
""
"Use real examples, not technical jargon."
""
"Instead of: “We need to extract that code from our Java monolith to a new microservice, there was a decision to start breaking it down to microservices”."
""
"Go for: “We want to move this logic to a new service our team will own, instead of the shared code. This will allow us to move much faster while implementing future requirements in that area, without affecting or depending on other teams”."
""
"When they challenge you and ask "why do we need this refactor" - stop and think, don't be defensive.

It's so easy (and tempting) to complain about people behind their backs, but do nothing about it."
""

""

Why I believe EVERY engineering manager should write code (please read till the end before commenting):"
""
"First, what I DON'T mean:"
""
"Releasing code to production every week, being part of each effort, or coding because it's what comes easy to you."
""
"Engineering managers SHOULD focus most of their efforts on the bigger picture."
""
"Yes, your coding time WILL decrease as you manage more people, and become manager of managers. That's ok."
""
"My argument is that you should not let it reach 0%."
""
"I believe you should be doing an actual small coding task (not just reviewing a PR!) now and then."
""
"The frequency depends on how big is your team and how senior you are - for a team leader, it should be every week or two. For a CTO, maybe it's once a year!"
""
"Experiencing the lives of your developers for yourself once in a while can be CRITICAL for you as a leader."
""
"Here are things you will learn:"
"• How's the developer experience in the company?"
"• What's the current state of the CI/CD?"
"• Are the tickets clear enough?"
"• How easy is it to onboard in a new repo?"
"• How good is the documentation?"
""
"Of course, you can learn everything just by asking your people. But let me ask you this:"
""
"What would you think about a sales manager who never makes sales calls?"
""
"Or a customer support manager who never solves customer issues?"
""
"Or a head chef who never cooks?"
""
"I would think they are out of touch. How can you REALLY know what your team is going through, if you are never down in the trenches with them?"
""
"It's very common to have engineering managers and directors who don't code at all. People managers, with a background in coding."
""
"Don't get me wrong - they can be great managers!"
""
"Still, I believe they are missing out on a critical part of the role."
""
"As for the main objections:"
"• It's a waste of a manager's time"
"• There are things with better ROI"
"• The developers are better at it"
"• It's part of becoming more senior"
""
"My answer is simple:"
""
"𝗪𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗶𝘀 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗱𝗼𝘄𝗻𝘀𝗶𝗱𝗲 𝗼𝗳 𝗱𝗼𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗶𝘁 𝗳𝗼𝗿 𝟭-𝟮 𝗱𝗮𝘆𝘀 𝗮 𝘆𝗲𝗮𝗿?"
""
"If your answer is that it's too little, and you won't be able to do anything in that time - to me it's proof you've gone out of touch."
""
"The ROI here is not the code you wrote - but that you stayed relevant.

Your team is overloaded with work, and you got permission to hire. You can afford 𝟭 𝘀𝗲𝗻𝗶𝗼𝗿 𝗳𝘂𝗹𝗹-𝘀𝘁𝗮𝗰𝗸 𝗱𝗲𝘃 or 𝟮 𝗷𝘂𝗻𝗶𝗼𝗿 𝗳𝘂𝗹𝗹-𝘀𝘁𝗮𝗰𝗸 𝗱𝗲𝘃𝘀. Which do you choose?"

"It's an interesting interview question."

"There is very limited data here, so of course there is no right answer. "
""

""
"𝗪𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝟮 𝗾𝘂𝗲𝘀𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀 𝘄𝗼𝘂𝗹𝗱 𝘆𝗼𝘂 𝗮𝘀𝗸 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗶𝗻𝘁𝗲𝗿𝘃𝗶𝗲𝘄𝗲𝗿 𝗯𝗲𝗳𝗼𝗿𝗲 𝗮𝗻𝘀𝘄𝗲𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴? "
""

"1. Who do I already have on my team? In terms of seniority and other qualities (like the ability to mentor). "
""
"2. Are there fast-approaching critical deadlines, or 'feeling stretched thin' is a general overload of the team?"
""
"If I don't have any additional data, I will probably go with hiring 2 (strong!) juniors. "
""
"What's your take?

A life hack 99% of people miss: "
""
"Answering welcome emails from products or newsletters.  "
""
"Here are the people I talked to because I do:"
""
"- Clint Jarvis, the CEO of Roots. I answered the welcome email from the app, and it led to a great zoom call, 2 articles I did, and future collaboration with a company I really believe in!"

Being told your team is "not working hard enough" is so annoying. There are 3 main reasons people will think it:"
""
"𝟭. 𝗟𝗮𝗰𝗸 𝗼𝗳 “𝗵𝘂𝘀𝘁𝗹𝗲.” "
""
"The external (usually senior) person feels that there are some things a 'hard-working team' should do, such as getting in early, going home late, being publicly present in communication channels, or some other manifestation of “working hard.” "
""
" 𝟮. 𝗟𝗮𝗰𝗸 𝗼𝗳 𝘃𝗶𝘀𝗶𝗯𝗹𝗲 𝗼𝘂𝘁𝗽𝘂𝘁. "
""
"Maybe the team didn't deliver anything the other person could see for a while."
""
"This can be because of:"
"• Poor communication of the team's work"
"• Unreasonable expectations  "
"• Poor prioritization "
""
"This one is especially difficult in remote work! "
""
"𝟯. 𝗟𝗮𝗰𝗸 𝗼𝗳 𝗽𝗮𝘀𝘀𝗶𝗼𝗻. "
""
"The external person feels that the team is doing important work, but the team is not motivated by it. Maybe they find the project boring and it offers them little challenge and opportunities to learn -> so they conclude the team can work harder."
""
"--------------------------------------------"
""
"Basically in all cases, there is a mismatch between what the external observer expects to see from your team compared to what is actually happening. "
""
"It's 𝗰𝗿𝗶𝘁𝗶𝗰𝗮𝗹 for you as the leader of the team to not ignore those issues, saying things like "they don't know what they are talking about, it's their problem"."
""
"I know it's tempting (believe me, I've done it a few times), but it will just hurt your team in the long term. "
""
"You need to figure out ways to bridge that gap. "
""
"𝘐𝘯𝘴𝘱𝘪𝘳𝘦𝘥 𝘣𝘺 James Stanier'𝘴 𝘣𝘰𝘰𝘬 "𝘉𝘦𝘤𝘰𝘮𝘦 𝘢𝘯 𝘌𝘧𝘧𝘦𝘤𝘵𝘪𝘷𝘦 𝘚𝘰𝘧𝘵𝘸𝘢𝘳𝘦 𝘌𝘯𝘨𝘪𝘯𝘦𝘦𝘳𝘪𝘯𝘨 𝘔𝘢𝘯𝘢𝘨𝘦𝘳".

How to stop being a control-freak engineering manager in 3 simple steps: "
""
"1. 𝗦𝘁𝗼𝗽 𝗰𝗼𝗻𝘁𝗿𝗼𝗹𝗹𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗱𝗮𝘆-𝘁𝗼-𝗱𝗮𝘆"
"   "
"You’ve got to stop obsessing about your old work. You are not a developer anymore. "
""
"Do you know that PagerDuty channel you are constantly monitoring? You are not the only one who can do that :)"
""
"Here’s a simple solution:"
""
"Have a ‘𝘁𝗲𝗮𝗺 𝗿𝗲𝗽’ for each day of the week. They’ll be responsible for communicating with the support team, and handling any alerts and production incidents."
""
"At first, they’ll consult with you. Make sure to NEVER solve it yourself! Don’t touch their keyboard! Let them deal with it. "
""
"With time, you will not even know those issues are happening."
""
"2. 𝗦𝘁𝗼𝗽 𝗹𝗲𝗮𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗻𝗲𝘄 𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗷𝗲𝗰𝘁𝘀"
"   "
"I only recently found out that in Big Tech the engineering managers are not the ones leading the projects - the engineers are. "
""
"When a new epic arrives, decide who from the team will own it. "
""
"𝗧𝗛𝗘𝗬 will meet with the PM, they will do the design"
"𝗧𝗛𝗘𝗬 will create the Jira tickets. "
""
"Yes, they’ll need you in the first project. Probably in the second too. "
""
"By the third one, they’ll get so much better. Not like you, they’ll need to handle only one of those each time, and they will be able to focus on it 100%, doing BETTER work than you."
""
"3. 𝗦𝘁𝗼𝗽 𝗯𝗲𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗴𝗼-𝘁𝗼 𝗽𝗲𝗿𝘀𝗼𝗻"
""
"I know you love to be a ‘source of knowledge’. It’s nice when people always need you around."
""
"Well, it’s time to get rid of that. Divide the different applications between the people, and clearly state who owns what. Believe me, they’ll enjoy the added responsibility.

Humans are strange - most of us feel even more uncomfortable giving praise than giving feedback..."

What I wish somebody had told me during my first year as a manager:"
""
"Such a crazy storm, right? Much harder than you expected."
""
"I know you feel guilty 90% of the time."
""
"- For cancelling 1:1s."
"- For being involved in every decision."
"- For firing Harry, as he was a nice guy."
"- For taking too long to fire Harry, as he didn’t give a shit."
"- For being too easy on standards, letting shitty code get into production."
"- For not having regular team meetings like you planned to."
"- For not getting along with your PM, when you know you should try harder."
""
"And that’s just a small part of your list."
""
"You feel like EVERY. SINGLE. THING. is on your shoulders."
""
"How the hell are you supposed to find the time to interview and hire, train people, perform code reviews, deal with production problems, have 1:1s, work with your PM, create a long-term vision, manage the scrum, and still find the time to lead new projects and write code!"
""
"You didn’t expect the emotional drain, right? "
""
"That some days you’ll be so exhausted from being responsible for everything, that you’ll come home and just collapse on the couch."
""
"Believe it or not, this is what EVERY engineering manager is going through. It gets easier in time. You get tougher, your people get better, and suddenly it’ll even feel too boring. In a year you’ll start pestering your manager for another promotion. You’re a crazy bastard."
""
"But to get there, there are 2 things you’ve got to stop doing! Yes, just 2:"
""
"1. Stop being involved in everything"
"2. Stop complaining about people behind their backs and doing nothing about it"
""
"Do you want to know how exactly to do that?"
""
"Great question - I wrote that article just for you:"
"https://lnkd.in/dxFU5zYd"
""
"Check if out of you sympathize with that :)

You sent a message to one of your remote devs (during working hours), and they answered only 3 hours later. What do you do?"
""
"I know most of the comments will be from angry developers, who'll say: "
""
""What's the problem? You need to trust your team, as long as they deliver on time stop micromanaging them". "
""
"The managers in the crowd will know it's not that simple. "
""
"What if another teammate is stuck and waiting for that employee?"
""
"Or you want to consult them before a decision you need to make?"
""
"The other extreme answer is "I expect employees to be 100% available during their working hours, and tell me in advance if not". "
""
"This loses the biggest advantage of working from home - the flexibility. You can pick up your kids from the daycare, eat lunch with your spouse, or just take a walk to refresh your mind. "
""
"Here's my answer from my Manager's ReadMe:"
""
""Please, when you are not working for more than an hour during working hours, put ‘out of office’ in your calendar. No need to ask permission or share the reason. Same for 'focus time' - if you want to code without distractions, just put it in the calendar before you start. I’ll know not to bother you."
""
"The reason is that sometimes people depend on you, and it’s nicer for everyone to know when a response is expected.""
""
"Is this micromanagement? Probably, for some people. "
""

The death of the hands-on engineering manager:"
""
"There are 2 types of engineering managers: Hands-on managers, and full-time managers."
""
"1. Hands-on ones take tasks in every sprint, know the codebase, and help the team fix hard bugs."
""
"2. Full-time managers attend meetings all day, have 1:1s, and have zero coding time."
""
"The sad truth is that almost all of us start from type 1, and slowly become type 2."
""
"I’ve been through that transition twice. When you are first promoted, you are close to the code. You were responsible for big parts of the codebase, and it’s easy to stay a part of the sprint."
""
"For the first couple of years, you code 30-70% of your time, with a slow decrease as your team grows."
""
"Then comes a sprint where you just have too many things. "
""
"Tons of meetings, a new project you are leading, the usual stuff. So for the first time, you decide to not take on any coding task."
""
"One sprint becomes 2, then 5, then 10."
""
"Suddenly a year went by and you barely opened your IDE. Now it’s not a question of having no time - it’s a habit you have lost. Even the most busy engineering managers can find 2-3 hours a week to do some coding."
""
"Getting back into the game is hard. You tell yourself “I can’t do anything useful in 2 hours. I’ll get into the zone and I will be distracted anyway. Better go over that document one more time”."
""

There are 2 approaches to being a manager: the 𝗦𝗮𝗳𝗲 𝘄𝗮𝘆, and the 𝗔𝗹𝗹 𝗜𝗻 𝘄𝗮𝘆. They don’t work well together:"
""
"𝗔 𝗦𝗮𝗳𝗲 𝗘𝗻𝗴𝗶𝗻𝗲𝗲𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗠𝗮𝗻𝗮𝗴𝗲𝗿:"
""
"You have a good relationship with your manager. You respect each other, and when they recommend something you listen. You fully believe in “disagree and commit”."
""
"You like the structured process of Scrum. The daily standups, the planning, the retrospectives. You sleep well knowing each sprint will look exactly the same. "
""
"You understand the product manager has their job, and you have yours, so you trust they'll do the job well. They create the roadmap, and your job is to deliver it on time. "
""
"You have a good relationship with your people. You have weekly 1:1s. You ask a couple of questions about their family. If they ask for a raise, you mention it to your manager, and then respect whatever decision they make."
""
"Your mantra is: “𝗜’𝗹𝗹 𝗱𝗼 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗯𝗲𝘀𝘁 𝗜 𝗰𝗮𝗻 𝗶𝗻 𝗺𝘆 𝗮𝗿𝗲𝗮 𝗼𝗳 𝗿𝗲𝘀𝗽𝗼𝗻𝘀𝗶𝗯𝗶𝗹𝗶𝘁𝘆”."
""
""
"𝗔𝗻 𝗔𝗹𝗹-𝗜𝗻 𝗘𝗻𝗴𝗶𝗻𝗲𝗲𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗠𝗮𝗻𝗮𝗴𝗲𝗿:"
""
"Your manager thinks you are a headache. You always want something - push one of your agendas, better compensation for your people, a promotion for yourself. "
""
"Your PM thinks you are a complete pain in the ass. You challenge the product roadmap. You want to talk to customers and solve their problems. You demand a high standard, and want to know exactly what's the goal of each project."
""
"You are never satisfied with ‘high velocity’ and ‘delivering on time’. You are constantly challenging your processes and trying new things. You want to achieve business outcomes, not more story points."
""
"You’ll go through fire for your team. "
""
"There is no such thing as “no” if their interests are involved. No budget for salary increases? Well, your manager will have to find some. You don’t just help them figure out career paths, you actually do something about that. If you need to create an opening, you’ll harass everyone until it happens."
""
"You expect a lot, and don’t give up if it’s uncomfortable. You are in  a constant battle to change things, creating chaos."
""
"You just truly, deeply, care."
""
"Your mantra is: “𝗜’𝗹𝗹 𝗱𝗼 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗮𝗯𝘀𝗼𝗹𝘂𝘁𝗲 𝗯𝗲𝘀𝘁 𝗜 𝗰𝗮𝗻”."
""
""
""
"I’m often ridiculed for being an All-Inner:"
""
"- “You are not a founder, why do you care so much?”"
"- “Let your people fight their own fights”"
"- “You cannot change everybody”"
"- “You’ve got to accept a ‘no’”"
"- “Just relax a little”"
""
"I know people are different, everyone has their own approach, and I should stop trying to change people. It doesn’t help."

"That’s me. That’s how I work. That’s how I live. I can’t just stop caring."

"All-Inners - don't let safers put out your fire. "

One of your developers always finishes the sprint a few days early. What do you do?"
""
"There are 3 common answers:"
"1. I just give them another ticket, what's to think about?"
"2. They can chill or work on something they care about"
"3. I'll ask them to help other team members"
""
"It's trickier than it looks."
""
"There are usually 2 different camps:"
""
"Camp 1: measure time. You should work X hours a week - so if you finish something faster than we thought, you'll get the next item in line. "
""
"Camp 2: measure output. If you commit to Y and finish, I don't care what you do with the rest of your time. "
""
"People love to say that camp 2 is the right answer to everything. "
""
""If someone takes 2 hours to finish a task that takes others a day, they earned the right to slack off. And if you want them to provide X4 output - pay them an X4 salary!". "
""
"That's BS. It's not practical in 95% of the places. "
""
"My take is to '𝘀𝗽𝗹𝗶𝘁 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗴𝗮𝗶𝗻'. "
""
"People should have an incentive to finish early. If someone got into the flow and worked late at night to finish a task - they shouldn't be punished. "
""
"My answer would be:"
""
""First of all, they earned the right to relax. Depending on the situation, I'll ask them to help other team members or give them a new task, but I'll make it clear they can take it easy, and there is no expectation to finish the new load"."
""

The 'it's important to me' card is a very simple trick for having a great relationship with your PM:"
""
"Here's how Will Guidara explained it in 𝘜𝘯𝘳𝘦𝘢𝘴𝘰𝘯𝘢𝘣𝘭𝘦 𝘏𝘰𝘴𝘱𝘪𝘵𝘢𝘭𝘪𝘵𝘺:"
""
""Sometimes, the only way to proceed in pursuit of a good partnership is to 𝗱𝗲𝗰𝗶𝗱𝗲 𝘁𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝘄𝗵𝗼𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗿 𝗰𝗮𝗿𝗲𝘀 𝗺𝗼𝗿𝗲 𝗮𝗯𝗼𝘂𝘁 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗶𝘀𝘀𝘂𝗲 𝗰𝗮𝗻 𝗵𝗮𝘃𝗲 𝘁𝗵𝗲𝗶𝗿 𝘄𝗮𝘆."
" "
"It wasn’t that I didn’t care about [ommited]… But it was 𝗺𝗼𝗿𝗲 𝗶𝗺𝗽𝗼𝗿𝘁𝗮𝗻𝘁 to Daniel than it was to me."
""
"There was an unwritten addition to this rule, which is that neither of us could abuse the “It’s important to me” card by pulling it too many times. "
""
"Mostly, though, we found that the willingness of the other person to relinquish their own position helped to build trust between us.""
""
"--------------------------------------------------------"
""
"Maybe there is a one-week-long refactor you want to do, but your PM doesn’t think is needed. "
""
"Or your PM wants to release an urgent fix, and you don’t feel it justifies working after hours."
""
"Give the other side some freedom."
""
"You’ll never agree on everything."
""
"You challenge each other, and have heated discussions, but you always know you have a card to pull for what’s truly important for you.

Imagine you are checking some dashboards and notice new signups dropped by 50%. What do you do?"
""
"Most people will start with:"
"❌ “I think I know what’s happening. The signup page might be down again; didn’t that happen last quarter? Or it could be related to that iOS update we shipped. But it’s also December, and it could just be a weak time for signups. Maybe it will go away on its own…”"
""
"What’s the problem with this? "
""
"𝗧𝗵𝗶𝘀 𝘀𝗲𝘁 𝗼𝗳 𝗿𝗮𝗻𝗱𝗼𝗺 𝗵𝘆𝗽𝗼𝘁𝗵𝗲𝘀𝗲𝘀 𝗱𝗼𝗲𝘀𝗻’𝘁 𝗵𝗮𝘃𝗲 𝗮 𝘀𝘁𝗿𝘂𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗶𝘀 𝗻𝗼𝘁 𝗰𝗼𝗹𝗹𝗲𝗰𝘁𝗶𝘃𝗲𝗹𝘆 𝗲𝘅𝗵𝗮𝘂𝘀𝘁𝗶𝘃𝗲. "
""
"If you get lucky, you find the problem; but if you’re not lucky, you might spend the entire day chasing down dead ends."
""
"And if you don’t finish the investigation quickly, you’ll likely 𝘀𝘁𝗮𝗿𝘁 𝗴𝗼𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗶𝗻 𝗰𝗶𝗿𝗰𝗹𝗲𝘀 since you lose track of what you already looked into."
""
"🎯 The solution: Break the problem into an issue tree that follows the MECE principle."
""
"------------------------------------"
""
"Yeah, I also have framework fatigue. Too many acronyms to remember, and most of them are useless in the day-to-day. "
""
"Last week, Jordan's and Torsten's article made me reconsider. "
""
"I promise you, it's quite genius:"
"https://lnkd.in/dRgTyAJp"
""
"𝘐𝘯 𝘵𝘩𝘦 𝘱𝘰𝘴𝘵, 𝘐'𝘮 𝘥𝘪𝘳𝘦𝘤𝘵𝘭𝘺 𝘲𝘶𝘰𝘵𝘪𝘯𝘨 1 𝘰𝘶𝘵 𝘰𝘧 5 𝘦𝘹𝘢𝘮𝘱𝘭𝘦𝘴 𝘧𝘳𝘰𝘮 𝘵𝘩𝘦 𝘢𝘳𝘵𝘪𝘤𝘭𝘦. 𝘈𝘭𝘭 𝘤𝘳𝘦𝘥𝘪𝘵 𝘵𝘰 𝘑𝘰𝘳𝘥𝘢𝘯 𝘢𝘯𝘥 𝘛𝘰𝘳𝘴𝘵𝘦𝘯!"
""
"------------------------------------"
""
"𝗠𝗘𝗖𝗘 = 𝗠utually 𝗘xclusive and 𝗖ollectively 𝗘xhaustive."
""
"You break down the problem into non-overlapping segments, that cover 𝗔𝗟𝗟 𝗽𝗼𝘀𝘀𝗶𝗯𝗹𝗲 𝗼𝗽𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀. "
""
"You start by dividing the possible root causes into high-level groups. Then, you break these groups down further, ensuring every possibility is captured and none of the buckets overlap."
""
"✅ After: “There are 𝘁𝘄𝗼 𝗼𝗽𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀 for what could be happening: Either something is broken, or the data only makes it look like there’s a problem. If something’s broken, it could be isolated to a specific OS, or affect all devices. And the issue could be at any of the three steps of the signup process, so we’ll check them one by one.”"
""
"Don’t rely on luck to find the root cause. The MECE framework gives you a structure that ensures you will find it, and makes it easy to:"
""
"1. get everyone on the same page on what’s being investigated."
"2. divide the work between different people or teams."
""
"Another benefit of using this structure is that since you have an overview of all possible things you need to check, you can prioritize what to investigate first."
""
"For example, you can see that checking for a data issue or seasonality first can potentially save you a lengthy investigation."
""

"Stop using words like 'significant' or 'annoying' - Weasel words."

""
"Gilad also started posting on LinkedIn, worth following! "
""
"-----------------------------------------------------"
""
""In Amazon, I learned to never use "Weasel words"."
""
"I stopped using terms like "significant", "low latency" or "annoying." 𝗜𝗻𝘀𝘁𝗲𝗮𝗱, 𝗜 𝘂𝘀𝗲𝗱 𝘀𝗽𝗲𝗰𝗶𝗳𝗶𝗰 𝗺𝗲𝘁𝗿𝗶𝗰𝘀, like "P99 latency jumped 375% from 12ms to 45ms." "
""
"This required more upfront work, but led to discussing reality as it is, not as I imagined it to be."
""
"I was working on 𝗺𝘆 𝗳𝗶𝗿𝘀𝘁 𝟲-𝗣𝗮𝗴𝗲𝗿, 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗶𝘁 𝘄𝗮𝘀 𝗵𝗮𝗿𝗱 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗳𝗿𝘂𝘀𝘁𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗴. "
""
"My manager connected me with a mentor who taught me how to write like an Amazonian. She taught me all about weasel words, how to identify them, and how to avoid them."
""
"But what sticks with me to this day is actually all the 1:1 meetings with my manager. "
""
"I learned that weasel words don't only appear in business documents. They also appeared in how I gave feedback to my team members. I learned to do the homework so that I could give specific feedback."
""
"I stopped saying "You are too blunt in meetings." Instead, I said "In the meeting with Christopher you cut him off when he started suggesting the React-based solution. We missed an opportunity to check more approaches. I also noticed that Christopher stopped contributing to other meetings.""
""
"This feedback is factual and thus indisputable. It is really so easy for the other side to understand. It is the first step to actionable advice."

Hiring ONLY seniors is the worst policy in the software industry.  Every company has a different excuse:"

""
"𝗜𝗻 𝗺𝗲𝗱𝗶𝘂𝗺-𝘀𝗶𝘇𝗲𝗱 𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗽𝗮𝗻𝗶𝗲𝘀 - “we are going to grow very fast, we need engineers who can handle scale and faced such challenges before"."
""
"𝗜𝗻 𝗯𝗶𝗴 𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗽𝗮𝗻𝗶𝗲𝘀 - “our infrastructure is super complex, it’ll take juniors too long to ramp up”."
""
"That’s bullshit. "
""
"Programming is not rocket science."
""
"Some developers became seniors in < 3 years, some of them even without a degree. If you don’t believe me, go read the stories of Jordan Cutler and Ryan Peterman."
""
"I hired 4 junior engineers in the last 2 years, and 3 of them delivered great results almost immediately. "
""
"Yes, Sometimes you need to hire a senior with very specific knowledge, especially in bigger organizations. "
""
"If you want to make an IOS version of your app, I wouldn’t hire someone who never worked with Swift… Or if your performance is getting worse as you scale up and you want someone to handle that, an experienced engineer is a better choice."
""
"But for most teams working on a SaaS product, even having 50% juniors can work. While they need someone to learn from, great ones don’t need a lot of 1:1 time and attention."
""
"Instead of saying "we hire only seniors", consider switching to "we hire only highly autonomous people who take ownership of their work". Focus on your needs, not on the experience. "

How to give a senior leader feedback (without getting fired):"
""
"It's hard for me to stay silent when I see a senior leader making life harder for everyone."
""
"I used to believe that speaking up was always the best thing for the organization."
""
"Turns out that if you make people angry, it's even worse than shutting up..."
""
"If you want to do it effectively, I REALLY recommend reading Wes Kao's article:"
"https://lnkd.in/dmy7w4TU"
""
"She shared 5 simple and super effective steps:"
""
"1. Make sure 𝗬𝗢𝗨 aren’t the problem. "
"   "
"Make sure you are managing up, asking clarifying questions, and acting like an owner. Before you blame the leader, reflect on how you could behave differently to improve outcomes for both of you. "
""
"2. Use the “𝗘𝘃𝗲𝗻 𝗺𝗼𝗿𝗲” 𝘁𝗲𝗰𝗵𝗻𝗶𝗾𝘂𝗲"
""
"🚫"
"You: “We might need to give guidance to new hires.”"
"Senior leader: “You think I don’t know that I need to give new hires guidance? I obviously gave them guidance. GTFO.”"
""
"✅"
"You: “We may need to give 𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗻 𝗺𝗼𝗿𝗲 𝗴𝘂𝗶𝗱𝗮𝗻𝗰𝗲 to new hires.”"
"Senior leader: “Hmm yeah. I gave them some guidance, but that probably wasn’t enough.”"
""
"The framing of “even more” works so well because you’re speaking from a place of generosity and giving the benefit of the doubt."
""
"3. Use 𝘆𝗼𝘂𝗿𝘀𝗲𝗹𝗳 𝗮𝘀 𝗮𝗻 𝗲𝘅𝗮𝗺𝗽𝗹𝗲"
""
"🚫 “You should try X.”"
"✅ “I used to struggle with this, and when I tried X, it really helped.” "
""
"Not saying YOU are struggling with X, just saying I used to and what I did. And if that lesson applies to what you’re dealing with, that’s cool, but no pressure."
""
"4. Adopt 𝗱𝗶𝗽𝗹𝗼𝗺𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗰, 𝗰𝘂𝗿𝗶𝗼𝘀𝗶𝘁𝘆-𝗳𝗼𝗿𝘄𝗮𝗿𝗱 𝗹𝗮𝗻𝗴𝘂𝗮𝗴𝗲"
""
"These phrases help you ease into the suggestion in a way that feels non-threatening:"
""
"• "We might want to try…”"
"• “I wonder if...”"
"• "Perhaps we could...”"
"• “What are your thoughts on...”"
"• “One approach might be...”"
""
"You adopt the vibe of a helpful team member who has your leader’s best interest in mind and wants to serve your organization. "
""
"5. Bring 𝗱𝗮𝘁𝗮 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗲𝘅𝗮𝗺𝗽𝗹𝗲𝘀 to support your point"
""
"🚫 “The team needs clearer priorities.”"
"✅ “The team made amazing progress when we all focused on the website update last month. It might help to have one or two clear priorities for the team this month that everyone can rally behind.”"
""
"🚫 “We should spend more time training new people.”"
"✅ “When we were able to dedicate that first week to training Steve, he got up to speed pretty quickly. The bit of upfront time seemed to have paid off, and taking a similar training approach for our next hire could help them ramp up just as fast. What do you think?”"
""

90% of engineering managers fall into 1 of 4 classic traps:"
""
"• Control freaks - staying involved in every decision. "
"• People pleasers - involve the whole team every time."
"• Zero-confidence managers who depend on their boss."
"• Hands-off managers who don't care about anything. "
""
"I'm a control freak. It took me a while to understand that not everything should go through me…"
""
"Instead, we should use ALL 4 available options for decision-making:"
""
"1. 𝗦𝗼𝗹𝗼 𝗱𝗲𝗰𝗶𝘀𝗶𝗼𝗻𝗺𝗮𝗸𝗶𝗻𝗴 - 'I'll decide it myself'."
"2. 𝗣𝗮𝗿𝘁𝗶𝗰𝗶𝗽𝗮𝘁𝗶𝘃𝗲 𝗱𝗲𝗰𝗶𝘀𝗶𝗼𝗻 - getting other people involved."
"3. 𝗘𝗹𝗲𝘃𝗮𝘁𝗲𝗱 𝗱𝗲𝗰𝗶𝘀𝗶𝗼𝗻 - let your manager decide for you."
"4. 𝗗𝗲𝗹𝗲𝗴𝗮𝘁𝗲𝗱 𝗱𝗲𝗰𝗶𝘀𝗶𝗼𝗻 - one your team makes for you. "
""
"Great managers are comfortable with using any of the above."
""
"Involving your manager in critical decisions so they won't feel blind. "
""
"Deciding small things by yourself. "
""
"Consulting with your team when needed, but knowing when it's the time to cut the debate and decide. "
""
"Letting your engineers be responsible and deciding for themselves where it fits.   "
""
"The tricky part is to balance it all. If you get feedback that you are too controlling, it doesn't mean you should completely let go. "
""
"And if your manager says they want you to make more decisions yourself, it doesn't mean to completely stop consulting with them."
""
"Yeah, you've got a hard job :)

7 things I've learned from leading a coding bootcamp for 4 years and teaching 200+ software engineers:"
""
"1. More people than you think can become software developers. Huge motivation can cover 𝘢𝘭𝘮𝘰𝘴𝘵 any gaps in knowledge/analytical skills."
"    "
"2. Some people have amazing brains but are horrible teammates. This is almost impossible to change, 99% of those didn't finish the bootcamp. "
"   "
"3. Not all people should be developers. People are pushed by their families because of the money. If they hate every minute of the bootcamp, there is 0% chance they'll enjoy the career."
""
"4. 6 months is a TON of time if you use it correctly. So much time is wasted in a college degree (yes, I've been through one)."
""
"5. Coding is fun. The best way to teach people is to always keep it in mind. "
""
"6. The biggest drive for people to succeed is to be surrounded by like-minded people. Motivation is contagious. "
""
"7. It's ok to not go easy on our brains. People can code for 12-14 hours a day and still think clearly. Sometimes those last couple of hours, when you push yourself further than you thought you could go, are the best ones."
"

If your risk score is above 7.5, you see the world differently than most people. Test yourself:"
""
"Rate yourself from 1 to 10 in each category, with 1 being very risk-averse and 10 risk-inclined."
""
"1. 𝗣𝗵𝘆𝘀𝗶𝗰𝗮𝗹 𝗥𝗶𝘀𝗸𝘀. Activities that involve some risk of injury. Riding a motorcycle, river rafting, skydiving, etc. "

"2. 𝗖𝗮𝗿𝗲𝗲𝗿 𝗥𝗶𝘀𝗸𝘀. Job changes, taking on new responsibilities, or seeking promotions. "

"3. 𝗙𝗶𝗻𝗮𝗻𝗰𝗶𝗮𝗹 𝗥𝗶𝘀𝗸𝘀. Your risk tolerance in investing, borrowing, and lending money."
" "
"4. 𝗦𝗼𝗰𝗶𝗮𝗹 𝗥𝗶𝘀𝗸𝘀. Introducing yourself to someone you don’t know or putting yourself in an unfamiliar social situation even at the risk of embarrassment. "

"5. 𝗜𝗻𝘁𝗲𝗹𝗹𝗲𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗮𝗹 𝗥𝗶𝘀𝗸𝘀. Your willingness to study a difficult topic, pursue information that challenges your convictions, or read an intellectually challenging book. "

"6. 𝗖𝗿𝗲𝗮𝘁𝗶𝘃𝗲 𝗥𝗶𝘀𝗸𝘀. Risks such as painting, drawing, taking on a writing challenge, or pursuing an unconventional design. "
" "
"7. 𝗥𝗲𝗹𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀𝗵𝗶𝗽 𝗥𝗶𝘀𝗸𝘀. A willingness to pursue a new relationship, spend time with someone despite an uncertain outcome, or make a relationship commitment. "

"8. 𝗘𝗺𝗼𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝗮𝗹 𝗥𝗶𝘀𝗸𝘀. Willingness to be emotionally vulnerable. "

"9. 𝗦𝗽𝗶𝗿𝗶𝘁𝘂𝗮𝗹 𝗥𝗶𝘀𝗸𝘀. Willingness to place your trust in concepts that may be impossible to prove or that you do not fully understand. "

What does it feel like to be an engineering manager at Amazon:"
""
"Since the RTO announcement it became popular to write shit about Amazon, but it's still one of the best companies around the world."
""
"A few weeks ago, Gilad Naor shared with the 'Leading Developers' readers 3 lessons from his time as an engineering manager at Amazon."
""
"Here is the first one, read the full article here: https://lnkd.in/dHBxv2Ha"
""
"𝟭. 𝗦𝗼𝗹𝘃𝗲 𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗯𝗹𝗲𝗺𝘀 𝘄𝗶𝘁𝗵 𝗺𝗲𝗰𝗵𝗮𝗻𝗶𝘀𝗺𝘀"
""
"My first meeting at Amazon was baffling."
""
"I entered a room with about a dozen Software Development Managers (SDMs) and Product Managers (PMs). Everyone sat in silence and looked at their laptops or the printed papers they had before them."
""
"It was my second day at Amazon, and I picked up one of the extra sheets and started reading. After those first ten awkward minutes, everyone finished reading and the meeting began."
""
"Why do Amazon meetings always start this way? "
""
"This is a specific example of how Amazon approaches problem-solving. It is the "Good Intentions Don't Matter" approach."
""
"In a famous internal video, Jeff Bezos talks about why good intentions are irrelevant. When things don't work, it does not matter what people wanted. All that matters is fixing the problems with mechanisms."
""
"For example, many busy managers come to meetings without first reading the prep material. They all intended to read the 6-Pager before the meeting. They just got sidetracked by the latest emergency. Scolding the managers won't solve the problem. A mechanism will. This is why all Amazon meetings start with a study period. The remaining meeting time is then spent on high-quality decision-making."
""
"Meta solves the same problem by creating cultural norms. I can't count the number of times that I heard a senior leader cut off the presenter: "Stop, assume that everyone here already read the slides. Let's get to the point." This is a culture-first approach. I have seen this work well in some teams, and not work at all in other teams."
""
"At Amazon, the solution is part of the company's "operating system"."
""
"-------------------------"
""
"Anton here - by now everyone is familiar with the 6-pager concept, but that "operating system" thinking can be used almost anywhere. "
""
"Think about something annoying that your developers do, and how can you solve it."
""
"For example, I remember being annoyed that people don’t update the Jira tickets. Finally, after requesting multiple times, now we just take 20 seconds from the standup to update them. "
"

A "20% for tech debt" rule just doesn't work:"

"Yes, setting time aside is definitely better than nothing - but it's not the solution to all your problems."
""
"Engineering managers often fall into 3 main traps when implementing the policy:"
""
"1. Not explaining the value of the work"
"2. "We’ll fix it later" mentality "
"3. Ineffectiveness"

"𝟭. "𝗪𝗲’𝗹𝗹 𝗙𝗶𝘅 𝗜𝘁 𝗟𝗮𝘁𝗲𝗿" 𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗮𝗹𝗶𝘁𝘆"
""
"This one is probably the most dangerous. "
""
"Once your organization dedicates 20% of its capacity to tech maintenance, it becomes tempting to push things to customers even faster, assuming you will fix everything later in your '20% tech debt' time."

"You will not."

""

Developers so often work on completely useless features because we are the biggest expense in the company. "

"The logic usually goes: "we pay them so much, we better make sure they always have work to do!""
""
"That’s how we got to the feature-factory pattern - instead of focusing on validation and experimentation, companies focus on producing tons of features of questionable (in the best case) value."

"Turns out it’s the same in movie-making:"

""Any company’s profit margin depends in large part on how effectively it uses its people:"

Managing a good team is sometimes harder than managing a struggling one, and many good managers make this mistake:"

""
""When downsides exist with upsides (as they most often do), people are reluctant to explore what’s bugging them for fear of being labeled complainers. "

"...They like so much of what they are doing, it allows them to put up with the parts of the job they came to resent. "

""

"The reality is very similar to pirate adventure movies..."

"If the water is calm, and everyone is happy - the monster is preparing to attack 🙃"
""
"Managing and building a team is an ongoing process. "
""
"There is no 'end' goal you can aim for. "
""
"Even if you feel your team is doing great - proactively seeking problems (and doing something about them) is a critical part of your job. "

How to clean up your Gmail inbox in 5 minutes (and not by deleting everything 😂):"
""
"1. Create a temporary label called 'to-read', and move the first page (~50 emails at most) from the Inbox. We will return them later to the Inbox, so you could easily go over them."
""
"2. Create the 'to-keep' label."
""
"3. Select ALL the emails in your inbox."
""
"4. Bulk assign the 'to-keep' label to all of your emails. Note: DON’T use the 'move' action! If you have thousands of emails, it won’t work, gmail will just be stuck. "
""
"5. Remove all all the emails from the Inbox, using the Archive button. This doesn’t delete them! You’ll still be able to find them in the ‘to-keep’ folder, and using the search."
""
"6. Restore the emails from the 'to-read' folder to the inbox."
""
"That’s it, now your Inbox is clean!"

Have you ever seen a roadmap like this:"
""
"October:      Feature Y Phase 1"
"November:  Feature Y Phase 2"
"December:   Feature Y Phase 3"
""
"This is the most common interpretation of having an ‘agile’, ‘lean’, ‘startup-like’ (pick your favorite buzzword) mentality."
""
"On the surface, it sounds legit. Instead of releasing a huge feature all at once, you break it down into smaller parts. You understand what is the minimum viable product, and what can be done later."
""
"That's a problem."
""
"If you decide in advance how that feature will be developed, you will be forever biased to follow that roadmap."
""
"So when you get some customer feedback, 𝘆𝗼𝘂 𝗵𝗲𝗮𝗿 𝘄𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝘆𝗼𝘂 𝘄𝗮𝗻𝘁 𝘁𝗼 𝗵𝗲𝗮𝗿. 𝗔𝗻𝘆 𝗻𝗲𝗴𝗮𝘁𝗶𝘃𝗲 𝘀𝗶𝗴𝗻𝗮𝗹 𝗮𝗯𝗼𝘂𝘁 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗳𝘂𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲 𝗽𝗵𝗮𝘀𝗲𝘀 𝘄𝗶𝗹𝗹 𝗯𝗲 𝗶𝗴𝗻𝗼𝗿𝗲𝗱, 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗽𝗼𝘀𝗶𝘁𝗶𝘃𝗲 𝗼𝗻𝗲𝘀 𝘄𝗶𝗹𝗹 𝗯𝗲 𝗰𝗹𝘂𝗻𝗴 𝘁𝗼."
""
"When you have a well-defined ‘phase 2’ in the roadmap, it means that you already decided. The chances you will stop after phase 1 are very small. The same problem exists with MVPs and PoCs - if a ‘PoC with a first customer’ is followed in the roadmap by ‘Feature Y improvements’, you fall into the same trap."
""
"The point of being agile, lean, fast - is not only to release in smaller chunks. It is also to be able to change your mind."
""
"When you are consulted about a roadmap for your team, ask those questions, from the easiest to the hardest:"
""
"1. How are we going to 𝗺𝗲𝗮𝘀𝘂𝗿𝗲 this feature?"
"2. How is the 𝘀𝘂𝗰𝗰𝗲𝘀𝘀 of phase 1 / MVP / PoC defined?"
"3. What will happen if the criteria are 𝗻𝗼𝘁 𝗺𝗲𝘁?"
"4. How will the 𝘀𝗰𝗼𝗽𝗲 𝗳𝗼𝗿 𝗽𝗵𝗮𝘀𝗲 𝟮 be decided?"
"5. What can make us decide to 𝗡𝗢𝗧 𝗰𝗼𝗻𝘁𝗶𝗻𝘂𝗲 with this feature?"
""

How outsourcing almost destroyed Dell:"
""
"20 years ago, Dell found out that it could lower the cost of production by asking ASUSTek to make simple circuit boards inside a Dell computer, instead of them making it. "
""
"Dell then could go to Wall Street and show how it lowers the operating costs."
""
"One day, Asus came to Dell with an interesting proposition: "
""
"“We’ve done a good job making these little circuits for you, let us supply the motherboards for your computers, too. Making motherboards isn’t your competence - it’s ours. And we can make them for a 20 percent lower cost.” "
""
"The Dell analysts realized that not only could Asus do it cheaper but it would also allow Dell to erase all the motherboard-related manufacturing assets from its balance sheet."
""
"After it had been reorganized to accommodate this arrangement, Asus came to Dell and said, “We’ve done a good job fabricating these motherboards for you, why don’t you let us assemble the whole computer for you, too? Assembling those products is not what’s made you successful. We can take all the remaining manufacturing assets off your balance sheet, and we can do it all for 20 percent less.”"
""
"The Dell analysts realized that this, too, was a win-win. That process continued as Dell outsourced the management of its supply chain, and then the design of its computers themselves. Dell essentially outsourced everything inside its personal computer business—everything except its brand—to Asus."
""
"Then, in 2005, Asus announced the creation of its own brand of computers. Asus had taken everything it had learned from Dell and applied it to itself. "
""
"𝗙𝗮𝘀𝘁 𝗳𝗼𝗿𝘄𝗮𝗿𝗱 𝘁𝗼 𝟮𝟬𝟮𝟰 - 𝗔𝘀𝘂𝘀 𝗶𝘀 𝘄𝗼𝗿𝘁𝗵 𝗫𝟱 𝗺𝗼𝗿𝗲 𝘁𝗵𝗮𝗻 𝗗𝗲𝗹𝗹…"
""
"I'm not sure how to formulate the moral of that story, but I feel it's very relevant in the age of no-code and AI. "
""
"A successful company needs to have at least one thing it is very good at, which is hard for the competitors to replicate."
""

""
"𝟭. 𝗗𝗲𝗳𝗲𝗰𝘁𝘀 / 𝗥𝗲𝘄𝗼𝗿𝗸"
""
"work that was not done correctly the first time, due to poor testing practices, unclear definitions of done, ambiguous requirements, or inadequate mockups."
""
"𝟮. 𝗣𝗮𝗿𝘁𝗶𝗮𝗹𝗹𝘆 𝗗𝗼𝗻𝗲 𝗪𝗼𝗿𝗸 and Backlog Mismanagement"
""
"Any work that is awaiting further action -  review process, missing requirements, QA. Returning to them requires refreshing memory, and reorienting engineers' focus."
""
"𝟯. 𝗘𝘅𝘁𝗿𝗮 / 𝗪𝗿𝗼𝗻𝗴 𝗙𝗲𝗮𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲𝘀"
""
"Features or products that nobody needs or wants. Not only do they waste time and money, but they also introduce extra complexity and maintenance challenges."
""
"𝟰. 𝗛𝗮𝗻𝗱𝗼𝗳𝗳𝘀 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗞𝗻𝗼𝘄𝗹𝗲𝗱𝗴𝗲 𝗟𝗼𝘀𝘀"
""
"When information or projects are transferred across individuals or teams."
""
"𝟱. 𝗧𝗮𝘀𝗸 𝗦𝘄𝗶𝘁𝗰𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗖𝗼𝗴𝗻𝗶𝘁𝗶𝘃𝗲 𝗟𝗼𝗮𝗱"
""
"When engineers are assigned to too many projects, have too many meetings, and face numerous distractions."
""
"𝟲. 𝗛𝗲𝗿𝗼𝗶𝗰𝘀 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗥𝘂𝘀𝗵 𝗠𝗼𝗱𝗲"

"individuals being pulled from planned work to handle numerous incidents, push last-minute changes, or share domain knowledge they alone possess."

""
"when skilled team members are given precise instructions on WHAT, HOW, and WHEN to perform tasks, stifling their creativity and problem-solving abilities."

""

"creating solutions that are more complex than necessary, which may lead to users struggling to use the product (opportunity waste) or a too-complex tech stack, which must be maintained over time."

""
"Any extra tasks in the SDLC that do not add customer value, such as reviews, approvals, manual steps in release processes, and non-automated reporting."

""
"𝘛𝘩𝘦 𝘰𝘶𝘵𝘱𝘶𝘵 𝘰𝘧 𝘢 𝘮𝘢𝘯𝘢𝘨𝘦𝘳 𝘪𝘴 𝘵𝘩𝘦 𝘰𝘶𝘵𝘱𝘶𝘵 𝘰𝘧 𝘵𝘩𝘦 𝘰𝘳𝘨𝘢𝘯𝘪𝘻𝘢𝘵𝘪𝘰𝘯𝘢𝘭 𝘶𝘯𝘪𝘵𝘴 𝘶𝘯𝘥𝘦𝘳 𝘩𝘪𝘴 𝘰𝘳 𝘩𝘦𝘳 𝘴𝘶𝘱𝘦𝘳𝘷𝘪𝘴𝘪𝘰𝘯 𝘰𝘳 𝘪𝘯𝘧𝘭𝘶𝘦𝘯𝘤𝘦."
""
"𝘛𝘩𝘦 𝘲𝘶𝘦𝘴𝘵𝘪𝘰𝘯 𝘵𝘩𝘦𝘯 𝘣𝘦𝘤𝘰𝘮𝘦𝘴, 𝘸𝘩𝘢𝘵 𝘤𝘢𝘯 𝘮𝘢𝘯𝘢𝘨𝘦𝘳𝘴 𝘥𝘰 𝘵𝘰 𝘪𝘯𝘤𝘳𝘦𝘢𝘴𝘦 𝘵𝘩𝘦 𝘰𝘶𝘵𝘱𝘶𝘵 𝘰𝘧 𝘵𝘩𝘦𝘪𝘳 𝘵𝘦𝘢𝘮𝘴?"
""
"𝘛𝘰 𝘨𝘪𝘷𝘦 𝘺𝘰𝘶 𝘢 𝘸𝘢𝘺 𝘵𝘰 𝘢𝘯𝘴𝘸𝘦𝘳 𝘵𝘩𝘦 𝘲𝘶𝘦𝘴𝘵𝘪𝘰𝘯, 𝘐 𝘪𝘯𝘵𝘳𝘰𝘥𝘶𝘤𝘦 𝘵𝘩𝘦 𝘤𝘰𝘯𝘤𝘦𝘱𝘵 𝘰𝘧 𝘮𝘢𝘯𝘢𝘨𝘦𝘳𝘪𝘢𝘭 𝘭𝘦𝘷𝘦𝘳𝘢𝘨𝘦, 𝘸𝘩𝘪𝘤𝘩 𝘮𝘦𝘢𝘴𝘶𝘳𝘦𝘴 𝙩𝙝𝙚 𝙞𝙢𝙥𝙖𝙘𝙩 𝙤𝙛 𝙬𝙝𝙖𝙩 𝙢𝙖𝙣𝙖𝙜𝙚𝙧𝙨 𝙙𝙤 𝙩𝙤 𝙞𝙣𝙘𝙧𝙚𝙖𝙨𝙚 𝙩𝙝𝙚 𝙤𝙪𝙩𝙥𝙪𝙩 𝙤𝙛 𝙩𝙝𝙚𝙞𝙧 𝙩𝙚𝙖𝙢𝙨. "
""
"𝘏𝘪𝘨𝘩 𝘮𝘢𝘯𝘢𝘨𝘦𝘳𝘪𝘢𝘭 𝘱𝘳𝘰𝘥𝘶𝘤𝘵𝘪𝘷𝘪𝘵𝘺, 𝘐 𝘢𝘳𝘨𝘶𝘦, 𝘥𝘦𝘱𝘦𝘯𝘥𝘴 𝘭𝘢𝘳𝘨𝘦𝘭𝘺 𝘰𝘯 𝘤𝘩𝘰𝘰𝘴𝘪𝘯𝘨 𝘵𝘰 𝘱𝘦𝘳𝘧𝘰𝘳𝘮 𝘵𝘢𝘴𝘬𝘴 𝘵𝘩𝘢𝘵 𝘱𝘰𝘴𝘴𝘦𝘴𝘴 𝘩𝘪𝘨𝘩 𝘭𝘦𝘷𝘦𝘳𝘢𝘨𝘦."
""
""
"As a team leader, coding is one of the tasks 𝘄𝗶𝘁𝗵 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗹𝗼𝘄𝗲𝘀𝘁 𝗹𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗿𝗮𝗴𝗲. You may improve the team's output in the current sprint, but it does nothing to help it in the long term. "
""
"Other activities with higher leverage:"
"1. Mentoring employees"
"2. Improving the team's processes"
"3. Connecting the team to the business side "
"4. Creating a vision for the team (and a sensible roadmap)"
""
"Team leaders 𝘀𝗵𝗼𝘂𝗹𝗱 code, but not for coding's sake. If you choose tasks in your favorite repo, the benefit is minimal. "
""
"A better option is to look for opportunities to:"
"• Learn a new technology "
"• Understand a process you are less familiar with"
"• Get a feeling of the current CI/CD process and its challenges "
""
"P.S.: How many times were you told to stop coding? 🙃

Why nice managers are so often BAD managers:"
""
"Because they want to spare your feelings, so they don’t tell you something that you absolutely need to know."
""
"The book that had the most impact on my behavior as a manager is 𝙍𝙖𝙙𝙞𝙘𝙖𝙡 𝘾𝙖𝙣𝙙𝙤𝙧, by Kim Scott. "
""
"It made me understand that what I considered to be ‘nice’ and ‘considerate’, is actually called “ruinous empathy”, and it hurts my team."
""
"Scott divides managers into 4 main types, from worst to best:"
"1. Manipulative Insincerity"
"2. Ruinous Empathy"
"3. Obnoxious Aggression"
"4. Radical Candor"
""
"𝗢𝗯𝗻𝗼𝘅𝗶𝗼𝘂𝘀 𝗔𝗴𝗴𝗿𝗲𝘀𝘀𝗶𝗼𝗻, also called brutal honesty or front stabbing, is what happens when the manager challenges someone directly, but doesn’t show them that he cares about them personally. It’s praise that doesn’t feel sincere or criticism and feedback that isn’t delivered kindly."
""
"Example:"
"Imagine getting this code review comment: "𝘛𝘩𝘪𝘴 𝘱𝘢𝘳𝘵 𝘭𝘰𝘰𝘬𝘴 𝘭𝘪𝘬𝘦 𝘴𝘩𝘪𝘵, 𝘳𝘦𝘸𝘳𝘪𝘵𝘦 𝘪𝘵 𝘪𝘯 𝘢 𝘸𝘢𝘺 𝘵𝘩𝘢𝘵 𝘩𝘶𝘮𝘢𝘯𝘴 𝘸𝘪𝘭𝘭 𝘣𝘦 𝘢𝘣𝘭𝘦 𝘵𝘰 𝘳𝘦𝘢𝘥.""
""
"This approach challenges the developer directly but fails to show any personal care or guidance on how to improve."
""
"𝗧𝗵𝗶𝘀 𝗮𝗽𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗮𝗰𝗵 𝗶𝘀 𝗕𝗘𝗧𝗧𝗘𝗥 𝘁𝗵𝗮𝗻 𝗿𝘂𝗶𝗻𝗼𝘂𝘀 𝗲𝗺𝗽𝗮𝘁𝗵𝘆! This was a huge surprise for me. Turns out people prefer such bosses, and respect them more, as they push them to succeed. They might like the “Ruinous Empathy” boss more, but in hindsight, they would usually prefer to work under the Brutal & Honest one."
""
"Edit: the best is of course a combination of both - an honest and nice manager, who cares about you but challenges you directly. "
""
"For a deeper dive, and tips on how to implement Radical Candor in your team, read the article my friend Orel wrote :)"
"https://lnkd.in/eWQECYBi

Competition is GOOD for your team. Don't grab your pitchforks yet, let me explain:"
""
"Often, competitive people are great engineers. "
""
"They have a drive to be better than everyone else, and they work very hard to improve. "
""
"I've covered the topic in depth in my article:"
"https://lnkd.in/dFFwkmv8"
""
"Here is the short version - there are 4 ways you can use it to your advantage:"
""
"1. 𝗕𝗲𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗽𝗲𝘁𝗶𝘁𝗶𝘃𝗲 𝗮𝘀 𝗮 𝗧𝗘𝗔𝗠"
""
"Around a year ago, I wanted to spice things up, so I made a bet with my manager. I bet that if we finish a project a week earlier than we promised - we  get an afternoon out as a team."
""
"The point was not to work more hours, but to be more focused - and it worked well!"
""
"Don't compete against other teams, but against a challenge - deliver a feature faster, improve performance by X%."
""
"2. 𝗖𝗼𝗺𝗽𝗲𝘁𝗶𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗜𝗡𝗦𝗜𝗗𝗘 𝘆𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝘁𝗲𝗮𝗺 "
""
"Junior engineers who arrive at a high-performing team will usually feel the urge to step up faster, to be up to standard. "
""
"A few years ago, I started a job with a long (3+ months) onboarding process. I did it in parallel to other developers, and it gave me a drive to do better and increase my pace."
""
"I LOVE any sort of competition. It gets the best out of me. But I know that each person is wired differently, and for a lot of people it can be an awful experience - so be careful with this one."
""
"3. 𝗗𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗹𝗼𝗽𝗲𝗿𝘀 𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗽𝗲𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘄𝗶𝘁𝗵 𝗧𝗛𝗘𝗠𝗦𝗘𝗟𝗩𝗘𝗦 "
""
"If you have competitive developers on your team, it will usually manifest itself in:"
""
"• Scope of work - bigger and harder projects."
"• Promotions - they’ll fight hard for that."
"• Salary increases."
""
"Embrace it and help them achieve those goals. "
""
"A fun and harmless competition for a junior developer could be reducing the amount of comments in PRs. Challenge them to find the most strict reviewer, and get the minimum comments. It’s fun to see the progress with time."
""
"4. 𝗕𝗲𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗽𝗲𝘁𝗶𝘁𝗶𝘃𝗲 𝗮𝘀 𝗮 𝗖𝗢𝗠𝗣𝗔𝗡𝗬"
""
"This is the BEST expression of competitiveness. When your whole company feels like a big  team - it’s you against the world (and your competitors)."
""
"In my opinion, it requires:"
"• Well-defined metrics"
"• Making the metrics highly visible to everyone "
"• Tell people how THEY contribute to those metrics "
"• Celebratemilestones!"
""
"You’ll see, competitive people will start to be obsessive about those metrics."
""
"----------------------------------------------------"
""
"Being competitive is neither good nor bad. It’s just who you are. Maybe people who hate competition are happier. I wouldn’t know 🤷‍♂️"
""
"In my opinion, it's just another tool we can use for our benefit. "
""
"* If you want to throw stones at me, please read the full article first :)

How to deal with fake urgency and not burn out your whole team:"
""
"Start with asking 3 questions."
""
"1. 𝗜𝘀 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗱𝗲𝗮𝗱𝗹𝗶𝗻𝗲 𝗿𝗲𝗮𝗹𝗹𝘆 𝗻𝗲𝗰𝗲𝘀𝘀𝗮𝗿𝘆?"
"   "
"When we are going into ‘all-in’ mode, there is a price to pay:"
""
"• You’ll accumulate technical debt"
"• Wrong decisions will be made"
"• People will burn out faster"
""
"Ask the hard questions. "
""
"Maybe the feature is not that urgent? Who decided that 2 weeks is a reasonable deadline? Does everyone understand that the quality will suffer?"
""
"You don’t have to like the answers. But when you ask your team to work very hard, they better know why."
""
"2. 𝗪𝗵𝗼 𝘀𝗵𝗼𝘂𝗹𝗱 𝘄𝗼𝗿𝗸 𝗼𝗻 𝘁𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗲𝗳𝗳𝗼𝗿𝘁?"
""
"It’s tempting to just throw everyone on it."
""
"For small projects, there is a limit to how many developers we can throw on it. "
""
"At regular times, we should use the optimal number. So if 2 people can finish a project in 2 weeks, and 3 people can do it in 2 days less, there is no sense in assigning the 3rd developer to that project full-time."
""
"In urgent times, we might decide to do that. Pair-programming, trying multiple solutions for the same problem simultaneously, or even working in shifts. "
""
"Still, there is a limit, above which additional people can even INCREASE the time it takes to finish."
""
"3. 𝗛𝗼𝘄 𝗵𝗮𝗿𝗱 𝘀𝗵𝗼𝘂𝗹𝗱 𝘄𝗲 𝘄𝗼𝗿𝗸?"
""
"Even in the most critical situations - people have lives. Families, kids, hobbies, responsibilities outside work. You can’t expect people to work 90-hour weeks and sleep in the office (unless you are the richest man on earth)."
""
"Explain WHY your team is going to work harder, and what’s so critical. Bring a senior leader to kickstart the effort (both to answer questions and boost morale)."
""
"And the MOST IMPORTANT QUESTION:"
""
"𝗪𝗵𝗲𝗻 𝗱𝗼 𝘄𝗲 𝗴𝗼 𝗯𝗮𝗰𝗸 𝘁𝗼 𝗻𝗼𝗿𝗺𝗮𝗹?"
""
"Someone might think: “Great work! Why don’t we always work like that? We have another urgent feature!”."
""
"And this is the point where the balance is broken. "
""
"It’s your job to set the boundaries and give some pushback where needed."
""

Stop overcomplicating Git!"
"99% of the time, you need only 7 commands."
""
"Let's say you are on the master branch, and have some local configurations."
""
"Before your work:"
"• git stash - to not lose them"
"• git pull - get remote updates"
"• git stash apply* - restore changes"
"  "
"Then:"
"• git checkout -b feature-branch"
"• -----DO THE WORK------"
"• git add . (requires a good .gitignore file)"
"• git commit -m "Added skeleton for feature""
"• git push**"
""
"* Thanks Orel for the tip about apply vs pop"
"** In the first time it will fail, and suggest the fix:"
"git push --set-upstream origin BRANCH-NAME"
""
"THAT'S IT!"
""
"The cases where you need rebase, cherry pick, reset and all their friends - are rare."
""
"Any command I missed?"
""
"#leadingdevelopers "
"#git

""
"You might think that k8s knowledge is not relevant."

""
"Having a better understanding of k8s and kubectl:"
"• Enables you to be less dependent on DevOps "
"• Helps you debug issues much faster "

""
"𝟰 𝘀𝗶𝗺𝗽𝗹𝗲 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝘂𝘀𝗲𝗳𝘂𝗹 𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗺𝗮𝗻𝗱𝘀:"
""
"1. 𝘬𝘶𝘣𝘦𝘤𝘵𝘭 𝘨𝘦𝘵 𝘱𝘰𝘥𝘴 -𝘯 𝘕𝘈𝘔𝘌𝘚𝘗𝘈𝘊𝘌 | 𝘨𝘳𝘦𝘱 𝘠𝘖𝘜𝘙_𝘚𝘌𝘙𝘝𝘐𝘊𝘌"
""
"In K8s, we usually need to work with specific pods. The ‘get pods’ command lets us see all active ones."
""
"2. 𝘬𝘶𝘣𝘦𝘤𝘵𝘭 𝘦𝘹𝘦𝘤 𝘗𝘖𝘋_𝘕𝘈𝘔𝘌 -𝘯 𝘕𝘈𝘔𝘌𝘚𝘗𝘈𝘊𝘌  -- 𝘦𝘯𝘷 | 𝘨𝘳𝘦𝘱 𝘚𝘌𝘊𝘙𝘌𝘛_𝘕𝘈𝘔𝘌"
""
"The ‘exec’ command lets us enter the pod itself, and run commands inside. "
"That’s SUPER useful when you want to verify which credentials you are actually using!"
""
"3. 𝘬𝘶𝘣𝘦𝘤𝘵𝘭 𝘦𝘹𝘦𝘤 𝘗𝘖𝘋_𝘕𝘈𝘔𝘌 -𝘪𝘵 -𝘯 𝘕𝘈𝘔𝘌𝘚𝘗𝘈𝘊𝘌 -- /𝘣𝘪𝘯/𝘣𝘢𝘴𝘩"
""
"When using ‘-it’, the exec commands starts an interactive shell for us. It means we can do stuff INSIDE the container!"
""
"4. 𝘬𝘶𝘣𝘦𝘤𝘵𝘭 𝘥𝘦𝘴𝘤𝘳𝘪𝘣𝘦 𝘱𝘰𝘥 𝘗𝘖𝘋_𝘕𝘈𝘔𝘌 -𝘯 𝘱𝘳𝘰𝘥𝘶𝘤𝘵𝘪𝘰𝘯 "
"   "
"Sometimes, our pods our failing, and the information in the GUI is not helpful enough. ‘Describe’ lets you see exactly what happens to the pod and why. "
""

No blame == No responsibility. "
""
"I don't believe in "Blameless" Culture."
""
"Let's take the case of a developer doing something wrong in the cloud, causing a 1-hour downtime for the whole organization."
""
"* 𝘛𝘩𝘪𝘴 𝘪𝘴 𝘯𝘰𝘵 𝘢 𝘩𝘺𝘱𝘰𝘵𝘩𝘦𝘵𝘪𝘤𝘢𝘭 𝘦𝘹𝘢𝘮𝘱𝘭𝘦. 𝘐 𝘩𝘢𝘥 𝘢 𝘣𝘢𝘥 𝘥𝘢𝘺..."
""
"I saw a few posts about the 'blameless postmortem'. "
"It's about finding the root cause instead of blaming."
""
"The idea makes sense. "
""
"But when the commercial team asks why the downtime happens, that approach can lead to messages such as:"
""We had an infrastructure problem". "
""
"Nobody takes responsibility. "
""
"But who should?"
""
"𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗹𝗲𝗮𝗱𝗲𝗿𝘀. Each in their own scope."
""
"* At the R&D level - the team leader."
"* At the company level - the VP R&D."
"* In outside communications - the CEO."
""
"And it's not fake! "
"It's not about 'shielding' the developer!"
""
"People shouldn't be afraid to make mistakes, it's part of the job."
""
"Such mistakes are usually the cause of too many permissions, not enough QA, or missing processes."
""
"But we should encourage, by example, 𝘁𝗮𝗸𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗼𝘄𝗻𝗲𝗿𝘀𝗵𝗶𝗽 𝗼𝗳 𝘁𝗵𝗼𝘀𝗲 𝗺𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗮𝗸𝗲𝘀. "
""
"If you are a team leader - nobody should care who's responsible for the actual mistake. "
"YOU are responsible for it."
""
"When each leader takes full ownership, there is a much higher chance that such issues will not happen again."
""
"𝘐 𝘩𝘪𝘨𝘩𝘭𝘺 𝘳𝘦𝘤𝘰𝘮𝘮𝘦𝘯𝘥 𝘳𝘦𝘢𝘥𝘪𝘯𝘨 "𝘌𝘹𝘵𝘳𝘦𝘮𝘦 𝘖𝘸𝘯𝘦𝘳𝘴𝘩𝘪𝘱", 𝘸𝘩𝘦𝘳𝘦 𝘵𝘩𝘪𝘴 𝘪𝘴 𝘥𝘪𝘴𝘤𝘶𝘴𝘴𝘦𝘥 𝘪𝘯 𝘮𝘰𝘳𝘦 𝘥𝘦𝘵𝘢𝘪𝘭 𝘪𝘯 𝘵𝘩𝘦 𝘧𝘪𝘳𝘴𝘵 𝘤𝘩𝘢𝘱𝘵𝘦𝘳"

Developers should never feel insecure or embarrassed to ask a question."
""
"8 years ago, Google performed a deep research into 'what makes a team effective'?"
""
""We learned that there are five key dynamics that set successful teams apart from other teams at Google":"
""
"1. 𝗣𝘀𝘆𝗰𝗵𝗼𝗹𝗼𝗴𝗶𝗰𝗮𝗹 𝘀𝗮𝗳𝗲𝘁𝘆: Can we take risks on this team without feeling insecure or embarrassed?"
"2. 𝗗𝗲𝗽𝗲𝗻𝗱𝗮𝗯𝗶𝗹𝗶𝘁𝘆: Can we count on each other to do high-quality work on time?"
"3. 𝗦𝘁𝗿𝘂𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲 & 𝗰𝗹𝗮𝗿𝗶𝘁𝘆: Are goals, roles, and execution plans on our team clear?"
"4. 𝗠𝗲𝗮𝗻𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗼𝗳 𝘄𝗼𝗿𝗸: Are we working on something that is personally important for each of us?"
"5. 𝗜𝗺𝗽𝗮𝗰𝘁 𝗼𝗳 𝘄𝗼𝗿𝗸: Do we fundamentally believe that the work we’re doing matters?"
""
"In that EXACT order of importance. "
""
"A few months ago, I went over it with the team. "
"Each one gave a score for each of the metrics."
""
"You can see the results in the attached image. "
""
"There are multiple things that we need to improve."
""
"But I had one main takeaway:"
"𝗪𝗲 𝘀𝘁𝗶𝗹𝗹 𝗵𝗮𝘃𝗲 𝗮 𝘄𝗮𝘆 𝘁𝗼 𝗴𝗼 𝗶𝗻 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗽𝘀𝘆𝗰𝗵𝗼𝗹𝗼𝗴𝗶𝗰𝗮𝗹 𝘀𝗮𝗳𝗲𝘁𝘆"
""
"When your most important KPI is not super high - focus on it first. "
""

How I stopped being a PagerDuty slave:"
""
"In your team, who is handling alerts & production issues?"
""
"• The team leader?"
"• The most senior developer?"
"• The developer who is responsible for that area?"
""
"As a team leader, it's very tempting to do it yourself:"
"• You want to know what's going on"
"• Often you can solve issues the fastest "
"• You keep your team from being distracted"
""
"That was always my instinct."
""
"The support team was talking directly with me. "
"I constantly monitored the pagerduty channels."
"I took only minor tasks to have time to respond."
""
"And then we started with 'Team rep'."
""
"Every day of the week, a different developer is the rep."
""
"The rep is responsible for:"
"• ❓  Any requests/questions the support team has"
"• 🛠️ All production issues on that day"
"• 🔍 Checking all alerts"
""
"They are 𝗡𝗢𝗧 solving everything."
"But they must know 𝗛𝗢𝗪 things were solved."
""
"Advantages:"
"✅ Knowledge distribution between team members."
"✅ Nothing depends on the team leader."
"✅ Distribution of responsibility."
""

The 5 biggest excuses for not doing regular 1:1s:"
""
"• We sit in the same room, we talk all the time."
"• We talk informaly, I know what's going on."
"• The developers don't want it, I asked."
"• They have too many meetings."
"• I'm not their mother."
""
"The 4 reasons why people are actually not doing 1:1s:"
""
"• You think you'll have nothing to talk about."
"• You are worried about what they will think."
"• You are an introvert and don't like talking."
"• It is not conventional in your organization."
""
"By 1:1, I don't mean a meeting centered around:"
"❌ A status update."
"❌ Going over the sprint."
"❌ Help in solving a technical problem. "
""
"I mean:"
"✅ Getting to know the 𝗽𝗲𝗿𝘀𝗼𝗻"
"✅ Building a personal connection"
"✅ Talking about dreams, goals, and progress"
""
"----------------------------------------------------"
""
"In my first role - I avoided 1:1s."
""
"I didn't do any(!) meetings."
"Nobody around me did 🤷‍♂️"
""
"In my next role, I was afraid of them."
""
"I had to collect myself before each meeting."
"I was constantly worried about how it will go."
""
"And it was HARD."
""
"Some 1:1s lasted for 5-10 minutes."
"Some I canceled any time I could."
"Some were only about the sprint."
""
"It took time, but now they are my best tool as a manager. And I enjoy them!"
""
"----------------------------------------------------"
""
"6 tips for making it work:"
""
"1. ⏱️ Don't be afraid of finishing early. "
"Even 5-7 minutes can be meaningful, if you make it so."
""
"2. 🗓️ Have a weekly meeting in the calendar. "
"Even 30 minutes is good. Some people think it's too much. I strongly disagree. You'll see the effects, try it. "
""
"3. ❌ NEVER cancel a meeting due to stupid reasons."
"It's the last meeting that should be canceled. Unless you are on vacation, or it's a total disaster - don't skip it. "
""
"4. 💭 Share about yourself - personal life, struggles."
"If you want to get to know the other side, you have to open up. This build trusts over time. "
""
"5. 🕳️ Don't fall into the 'everyday' trap."
"It can be tempting to use the time to talk only about work stuff. While it's ok to use it like that, make sure to at least start with personal conversation."
""
"6. 🎯 Talk once in a while about professional goals."
"It'll help you understand if the day-to-day tasks are aligned with the person's goals. If it's easy/boring for too long - you have to make some adjustments."
""
"To sum up:"
"Take your 1:1s seriously. "
"You'll see the results.
