Ever tried sprinting in deep water?"
""
"That's how it feels when bottlenecks slow down our work."
""
"Thank god we have the 'Theory of Constraints'!"
""
"-----"
""
"One of the first books I listened to in Audible was 'The Goal'."
""
"It tells the story of Alex, a failing factory manager."
""
"Little by little, Alex uses the Theory of Constraints to turn everything around."
""
"𝗕𝘂𝘁 𝘄𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗶𝘀 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗧𝗵𝗲𝗼𝗿𝘆 𝗼𝗳 𝗖𝗼𝗻𝘀𝘁𝗿𝗮𝗶𝗻𝘁𝘀?"
""
"I'll explain using this great story:"
""
"Alex takes his scout troop, including his son, on a hike."
""
"There is a very slow boy, called Herbie, who is always the first to get tired. "
""
"He walks in the middle of the group. "
""
"As they are walking one after another, and Alex forbade the kids to overtake each other, gaps in the column are immediately formed."
""
"Everyone is frustrated - the fast boys have to wait every few minutes, and Alex can't properly manage the group."
""
"Then he has an epiphany. "
""
"He realizes that if Herbie will lead the line, and they redistribute the stuff in his backpack to others, the group can get a good pace without leaving anyone behind."
" "
"This experience helps Alex to understand the problem he's facing at the factory. "
""
"He identifies the slowest machines (the "Herbies") in his production line and makes sure they are not overloaded and always have work to do, which optimizes the whole flow."
""
"This metaphor illustrates the Theory of Constraints' central principle: "
"• Identify your system's constraint (bottleneck)."
"• Exploit it (make sure it's always working)."
"• Subordinate everything else to it (adjust all other processes to support the bottleneck process)."
""
"-----"
""
"The idea is brilliantly simple. "
""
"Each company has its own bottlenecks."
""
"It can be:"
"- Technical debts (outdated technology)"
"- Slow feature discovery process "
"- Complex deployment process"
""
"Let's assume the biggest bottleneck a company has is a slow feature discovery process."
""
"Not enough features are ready for development."
""
"Based on the theory, they should:"
"• Find the bottleneck - why is it slow? Let's assume it's the PM's time."
"  "
"• Exploit it - make sure the PMs have no obstacles in their way."
"  "
"• Subordinate everything else - remove any other tasks from the PMs, and move them to other people. Make sure that everyone who can, helps in the discovery."
""
"What are your tips for removing bottlenecks?"
""
" #bottlenecks #theoryofconstraints "

What killing Cobras has to do with story point estimations?"
""
"It's called 'The Cobra Effect'."
""
"The British wanted to reduce the population of venomous cobras in India."
""
"To do that, they gave incentives for the snake hunt by offering rewards for dead snakes. "
""
"In response, Indian citizens started breeding snakes so that they’d have more to kill, and more rewards to reap..."
""
"---"
""
"It's an interesting concept, called 'Perverse incentive'. "
""
""An incentive that has an unintended and undesirable result that is opposite to the intentions of its designers"."
""
"You can see where this goes. "
""
"If we measure by 'Story points completed' - the immediate incentive is to give yourself tons of buffers. "
""
"Knowing they have extra time, people might feel less pressure to work efficiently."
""
"This will lead to the buffer being consumed even when it's not really necessary."
""
"I mainly encountered it in the army, where a common mentality is to do as little as possible."
""
"Where have you encountered the Cobra effect?

"The actual range is from 0% to 80%. "
""
"I've heard many 'rules-of-thumb.'"
""
"The one I remember the most - each developer you manage is one less day of coding. So with 3 - you can code 40% of the time. With 5+ - it's zero."

"• 𝗖𝗼𝗱𝗶𝗰𝘁𝘀 - developers who were 'pushed' to manage, and prefer coding. "

"They spend less time managing, but are integral to developing core features. "
""
"• 𝗠𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗼𝗿𝘀 - fresh managers, who want to be good managers for their employees, but still want to code a relatively high percentage of the time. "

"• 𝗗𝗲𝗹𝗲𝗴𝗮𝘁𝗼𝗿𝘀 - long-time managers or those who didn't enjoy coding that much from the beginning. "

""
"Currently, I'm in the middle of the shift from 'mentor' to 'delegator', but still holding on 🙃"
""
"I'm trying to stay at around 30%-40% (down from 60%-70% when I just started out).  "
""
"To all the 'mentors' out there. If you feel guilty for not being hands-on enough - you are not alone!"

"If you squeeze hands-on tasks to fill 30% of your time - you are already better than average. "
""
"#handson #leadingbyexample

Meta stock is up 𝟳𝟬% (!) this year. "
"Meta fired 𝟮𝟭,𝟬𝟬𝟬 employees in the last 6 months. "
""
"Yes, those stats are connected. "

"But wouldn't you expect the opposite? If a company has to fire 10K employees, twice, it means something is wrong?"

"Instead. You see sentences like:"
"𝘞𝘢𝘭𝘭 𝘚𝘵𝘳𝘦𝘦𝘵 𝘤𝘰𝘯𝘵𝘪𝘯𝘶𝘦𝘥 𝘵𝘰 𝘤𝘩𝘦𝘦𝘳 𝘰𝘯 𝘔𝘦𝘵𝘢’𝘴 𝘤𝘰𝘮𝘮𝘪𝘵𝘮𝘦𝘯𝘵 𝘵𝘰 𝘵𝘳𝘪𝘮 𝘤𝘰𝘴𝘵𝘴 𝘢𝘯𝘥 𝘪𝘮𝘱𝘳𝘰𝘷𝘦 𝘪𝘵𝘴 𝘣𝘰𝘵𝘵𝘰𝘮 𝘭𝘪𝘯𝘦."
""
"This is what Simon Sinek calls a "Finite Mindset.""
""
"Sinek talks in "The Infinite Game" about the difference between finite games – chess, football – to infinite games – education, marriage, etc. "
""
"Infinite games CANNOT BE WON."
""
"You can’t be ‘best at marriage’ or ‘win education.’"
""
"He advocates that companies are often run with a finite mindset. "
""
"The CEOs focus on decreasing costs (by firing people), meeting yearly goals, and making Wall Street happy. "
""
"Instead - they can take a short-term hit, to build something bigger for the future. "
""
"I'm not saying you should never fire employees. It just seems to me that the priorities are kinda messed up. "
""
"If I have to bet? Meta will be a shadow of itself by 2030.

"The public toilet exam", and what it has to do with success 🚽:"
""
"We all hate public toilets (except maybe in fancy airports)."
""
"You enter the restroom stall after someone. "
""
"You see some pee on the seat, and probably some toilet paper on the floor. 🧻"
""
"Do you think: "I can't believe that guy!"?"
""
"Anyway, you do your thing and exit the stall. "
""
"Would you clean it up, even if it's not your mess? "
""
"If not, someone else enters after you... "
""
"Guess what he will think?"
""
"When you enter a dirty stall after someone, your respect for him diminishes. I had this experience multiple times with high-ranking officers of my unit in the army. "
""
"A lot of the time, we are judged based on messes we didn't create. "
""
"It's ok to clean someone else's shit, quietly, without boasting. "
""
"I try to leave public toilets in a little better shape than I found them."
""
"It sounds very minor, but I think it's a good mindset. Thinking about who will come after you is relevant everywhere - code maintenance, design, leadership. "
""
"Leave a better legacy. Start in the restroom. 💩

""
"By averaging your work together with a small team, you can achieve better results than averaging everyone's work in a large company. "

Hate code reviews? "
""
"I can't promise you'll love them, but I can help you hate them a little less..."
""
"There are 4 types of code reviewers:"
""
"1. The "𝗦𝗸𝗶𝗺𝗺𝗲𝗿" 😎 - at best, you will get from him a couple of minor comments. Don't count on this one to find your bugs. "
"   "
"   The big advantage - with no comments, comes zero fixes! When you are in a rush, always look for the nearest skimmer. "
""
"2. The '𝗚𝘂𝗿𝘂' 🧠- prepare to get your code (and self-esteem) bombed with comments. She will get into the deepest part of your soul (code), find all the weaknesses, and teach you how to code like a pro. "
"   "
"   Be ready to spend 3 days fixing the issues..."
"     "
"3. The '𝗣𝗲𝗿𝗳𝗲𝗰𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝗶𝘀𝘁' 🔍- you will get 50 comments, all over the place. Your variables are badly named, not enough explanations, too many functions, not enough tests."
"   "
"   You'll fix everything. "
"   "
"   Then you'll get 13 more comments. You'll fix them. "
"   "
"   4 more comments. Are we done yet??"
"   "
"   Always 1 last comment before the approval."
"   "
"   *If you are dealing with a guru-perfectionist, good luck..."
""
"4. The "𝗶𝗴𝗻𝗼𝗿𝗲𝗿" 😴 - you will not get your code reviewed unless you stand up, go to his office, take a chair, sit by him and ask nicely to do it RIGHT NOW. Then he usually becomes either a "Perfectionist" or a "Guru"."
""
"Tips for dealing with each type:"
"• Help the skimmer, highlight key areas 𝗶𝗻 𝗮𝗱𝘃𝗮𝗻𝗰𝗲, using comments. "
"• 𝗦𝗲𝘁 𝗹𝗶𝗺𝗶𝘁𝘀 with the perfectionist. It's okay to disagree on programming styles."
"• 𝗕𝗲 𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗮𝗰𝘁𝗶𝘃𝗲 with the ignorer. GitHub and Slack are not good enough - be assertive. "
"• Get the guru 𝗶𝗻𝘃𝗼𝗹𝘃𝗲𝗱 𝗲𝗮𝗿𝗹𝘆, even during the design phase. Share your work-in-progress code."
""
"Personally, I use the following approach:"
"• Usually I'm a shameless skimmer. "
"• Reviewing a Junior developer's code - a perfectionist. "
"• For code that will be 'the meat' of the system - I try to guru it. "
"  "
"Which code reviewer do you enjoy working with?"
""
"Tag them! (Just don't give away our cherished skimmers 🙃)"
""
"#codereview #programming, #developerlife

"- Don’t try to write fancy. Use your imagination or memory - write the truth."

""
"A 6th tip - write for yourself first.

"I find it hard to focus on a task for more than 10 minutes straight, and it is becoming worse.  "

""
"According to Cal Newport, deep work is where the real magic happens. It's where you're brain is fully engaged and working towards a goal that will make a meaningful impact. "
""
"Shallow work is all the mindless tasks that can be done half-listening to a Zoom call."
""
"Now, don't get me wrong, shallow work has its place. We all have to answer emails and attend meetings, but it's not going to be the work that gets you to your big goals."
""
"Start small:"
"• Set aside in Google Calendar a 'Focus Time', of 30-60 minutes."
"• Start with a time frame that is challenging but doable."
"• Don't touch ANY distracting app during that time."
"• Gamify it - try to progress as far as you can. "
"• Need help in Slack? Wait for the time to end."
"• Time's up! Take a break, some social oxygen. "
"• Repeat 5 times a week.  "
""
"For me, 2 hours of focus time are more productive than a full week without it. "
""

"For me, the sauna at the gym is my reward for completing my workout."

Could you compete in the Olympics if you began training at 4?"
""
"-----"
""
"I like to think that professional athletes, musicians, and painters are all part of a secret society where they're given magical powers at a young age."
""
"They spend their childhoods training tirelessly, waking up at the ungodly hour of 5am and sacrificing their social lives."
""
"Meanwhile, us regular people are left to wonder how they became so damn good at what they do."
""
"The '10000 hours' rule supports this thesis - practice well for 10,000 hours (4 hours a day, every day, 7 years), in anything, and you will be a true expert."
""
"A guy named László Polgár had the theory that anyone can be a genius if they start training early enough. So, like any good scientist, he decided to test his theory on his own children."
""
"And what do you know? The 3 Polgár sisters all became very successful chess players, with the youngest being considered the best woman player in history! Looks like Dad was right all along."
""
"-----"
""
"David Epstein's 'Range: Why Generalists Triumph In A Specialized World' rejects the idea that specializing early and hard is the key to success."
""
"Diverse experiences defeats deep expertise in one field. Generalists, with a range of skills and interests, can adapt better and succeed in our complex world."
""
"He divides skills into "Specialization" (like Chess and Golf) and "Range" skills."
""
"Roger Federer started playing tennis at 8 (twice as old as Nadal and Djokovic) and also played soccer, badminton, and basketball. This helped him develop a well-rounded skill set that served him well in his career as a specialist."
""
"-----"
""
"In my current role, I clearly see the benefit of my relatively diverse background."
""
"My time teaching programming in the army was boot-camp for my brain. It taught me how communicate with anyone, from a junior developer to a grandma who just wants to send an email."
""
"The experience as a DevOps team lead taught me to work under pressure and make quick decisions. The hands-on experience of managing databases and resolving issues in a timely manner honed my sense of urgency and ability to prioritize tasks."
""
"-----"
""
"The diversification Epstein talks about is much bigger - changing your career. Doctors who become entrepreneurs, engineers becoming teachers. "
""
"Changing careers is like trying to teach an old dog new tricks, but as Epstein points out, it's never too late to learn."
""
"In a world where technology is constantly evolving, having a diverse background as a software engineer can make you the next big inventor or leader.

Did you ever find yourself looking up the same question in Stack Overflow?"
""
"Or for an article you have read but can't remember where?"
""
"I want to tell you about a system of organizing your thoughts, work and life. "
""
"---"
""
"Here's the 18 minutes version, presented to Taranis TLV: http://bit.ly/3Hgtplg"
""
"---"
""
"We consume hundreds of pages of information per day from diverse channels: websites, blogs, social networks, podcasts."
""
"Keeping track of this information in our heads is impossible, but we still try. The price is frustration, stress and time wasted."
""
"The system suggested consists of 4 steps: Capture your information, Organize it for action, Distill the relevant parts, and Express your ideas. "
""
"It is based on the book Building a Second Brain: by Tiago Forte, and greatly improved my life."
""
"---"
""
"Luckily, we have our computers and cellphones at our disposal. Let’s enhance our brain, offload everything we can, and leave time and space for it to do what it does best - being creative. "
""
"The system"
""
"- Capture"
""
"The start is crucial. How can you find the information if you haven’t saved it? Any notes app will do - I’m using Evernote. "
""
"What should be saved:"
""
"#1 Does It Inspire Me? "
"#2 Is It Useful? "
"#3 Is It Personal? "
"#4 Is It Surprising? "
""
"- Organize "
""
"It’s time to organize the notes in a meaningful way. Save your notes for Actionability!"
""
"“Imagine how absurd it would be to organize a kitchen by kind of food: fresh fruit, dried fruit, fruit juice, and frozen fruit would all be stored in the same place, just because they all happen to be made of fruit. "
""
"This is exactly the way most people organize - keeping all their book notes together just because they happen to come from books"
""
"Instead of organizing ideas according to where they come from, I recommend organizing them according to where they are going.” T. Forte."
""
"Use the following structure: "
""
"#1 Projects: Short-term efforts in your work or life "
"#2 Areas: Long-term responsibilities you want to manage over time."
"#3 Resources: Topics or interests that may be useful in the future."
"#4 Archives: Inactive items from the other three categories."
""
"A useful tool to have is an ‘inbox’ folder, where you put all the notes, and sort them down when convenient. "
""
"- Distill"
""
"Every time you take a note, ask yourself, how can I make this as useful as possible for my future self? Use several layers of distilling - in bold relevant sentences, highlight relevant words. "
""
"Now your useful information is stored in a meaningful context and an easily readable way."
""
"- Express"
""
"Capturing your thoughts and ideas before starting the project, gives a big boost! You can easily overcome the 'blank page' syndrom this way. Once you have an 'Archipelago of Ideas', when you start to work, you just need to connect the dots."
""
"We are drowning in a stream of information. Our mind is constantly working, making us tired and irritable. The simple steps we went through, can allow you to be calmer, more patient, and better at any work you do.

Should I listen to my parents?"
""
""
"It's easy to get caught up in the trend of switching jobs every couple of years in search of new opportunities and growth."
""
"It's understandable - the salary is usually higher, and you get some new juice back into your life."
""
"Our parents' generation had a different approach to their careers, and they don't shy away from letting us know that. "
""
"They often stayed with the same company for their entire working lives, building relationships and climbing the ranks within that organization. But is this approach really outdated?"
""
"------"
""
"Bob Iger, CEO of Disney, knows a thing or two about career success. He worked his way up the ranks at Disney for 46 years before becoming CEO. "
""
"In his book "The Ride of a Lifetime," he shares his insights on the importance of a "pragmatic enthusiasm" when it comes to our careers. "
""
"He explains that even in the face of difficult choices and less than ideal outcomes, an optimistic leader does not yield to pessimism."
""
""
"There's no one-size-fits-all answer. We all have our ups and downs, and sometimes we do have to take a long break or switch jobs. But there are benefits to "toughing it out"."
""
"- Deeper professional understanding of the industry and organization, making yourself an expert."
""
"- Strong relationships, which can lead for new opportunities down the road (and good friends)."
""
"- Job security, especially in our current times. Employers are more likely to invest in the development and training of long-term employees, and are less likely to lay off or terminate these employees during difficult economic times."
""
"So, before you start sending out resumes and searching for that next big salary bump, consider the benefits of staying put and focusing on building relationships, expanding your skill set, and becoming an invaluable asset to your current employer. It could be the ride of a lifetime. "
"

"The leadership model we are most familiar with is the 'leader-follower' one, which is based on the personality of the leader. Your main goal as the leader is to deliver while you are there, without metrics for the future."
""
"It's rarely that:"
"💡 People are measured/rewarded for what happens in the organization after they transfer"
"💡 When an organization is doing worse immediately after a leader leaves, that leader is accountable"

"In his book 'Turn the Ship Around!', L. David Marquet suggests to move from a leader-follower model, to a leader-leader model. He shares his story as a Submarine captain in the US-navy, turning a poorly performing submarine to an award-winning one."
""
"Two main points I took:"
"💡 Share with the team your decision making process, expect from them to be owners of their part. When a team member finds a technical debt she wants to fix, or a feature to implement, expect them to come prepared. "I found we have this technical debt in service X, here's the ticket with the details, I would like to fix it next sprint"."
"💡 You shouldn't be the 'authority' in charge. Foster a culture of 'I intend to do X...", instead of asking permission from you, which causes you to ask probing questions (did you check this? are you sure of that?). For example, "I intend to run this migration on the DB, I checked it on QA and it works". "

"💰 What You Measure and Reward Is What You Get"
""
"Continental's planes were always late, which caused the company to pay $5M in fines each month. Bathune told the employees - let's improve that metric together, and share the profit."
"For every month that Continental was in the top 3 arilines on being on time - ALL employees got $65 bonus. The bonus was sent by a separate check in the mail - to emphasize the achievement."
"It worked."
""
"💡 Rewarding for performance is something we tie in our minds mainly with sales positions, less with programmers. We get our money, and do our job."
""
"Using rewards to steer the our team is a great tool, and it doesn't have to be money. For example - if you release a big feature on time, have a fun afternoon for everyone involved. Your recruitment team brought 20 candidates that passed the first interview? Let them celebrate with a fun activity!"
""
"It's also important to emphasize and celebrate the reward, not just add it to the paycheck. A weekend in europe for 2 is much more emotionally enticing than $2000 in the paycheck (for example as a reward for bringing a friend)."
""

""
"The book goes over all RnD roles - from developer and tech lead, to CTO and VP R&D, and provides meaningful insights the author has about excelling in each role."
""
"I'll outline the 5 lessons I took from the book, across 3 categories (as LinkedIn has a limit of 3000 characters per post :))"
""
"🧑​​ How to be managed"
""
"💡 Expect regular one-on-ones, ask for them if needed. Prepare an agenda, and guide your manager to where you need her most. "
""
"💡 Give your manager a break. The manager's job is not to make you happy all the time, but to do what's best for the company and the team. "
"Your relationship with your boss is like any other relationship - the only person you can really change is yourself (sorry Amir Zipori)."
""
"💡 This said - choose your manager wisely when switching jobs. He may have a huge effect on your career. As often said - people leave managers, not jobs. "
""
""
" "
"👨Being a manager"
""
"💡 DO REGULAR one-on-ones! They may be in different styles and lengths, but the most important thing is to give your employees a safe and predictable place to get your attention. Many people say 'I don't need those meetings', 'this is not my style' and 'I'm sitting with my people, we talk everyday', in my opinion those are "
"just excuses. "
"Starting doing one-on-one meetings when people are not used to doing it may be strange and uncomfortable. Suck it up, it'll become more natural. "
""
"👨‍🦳 Manager of managers"
""
"💡 The fallacy of an 'open door' - this I mostly encountered in the army, where senior officers had 'open door' hours, priding themselves for being accessible."
"Fournier claims that rarely real problems are revealed this way, or useful information learned. "
"It's very important to do proactive skip-level meetings, to talk to employees not directly reporting to you. It shouldn't undermine their managers if you do it properly, and provide you with a better picture of your organization. "
